title: Data Wrangling with tidyverse: A Practical Guide
text:
Step 4: Visualise the Clean Data


Code
clean_survey |>
  filter(!is.na(gender_clean), !is.na(employment_clean), !is.na(income_num)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = income_num, fill = gender_clean)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.6, adjust = 1.5) +
  facet_wrap(~employment_clean) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(prefix = "₹", big.mark = ",")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Male" = "#2563eb", "Female" = "#e11d48")) +
  labs(
    title = "Income Distribution by Gender and Employment Status",
    x = "Monthly Income (₹)", y = "Density", fill = "Gender"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "#f1f5f9", color = NA),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "top"
  )





Income distribution by gender and employment status

title: Data Wrangling with tidyverse: A Practical Guide
text:
Key Takeaways
The tidyverse pipeline (|>) allows you to chain data transformations in a readable, logical sequence. The key principles I follow:

Never modify raw data — always create a new clean_ object
Standardise early — fix encoding, case, and symbols before any analysis
Document decisions — use # comments to explain why you made choices
Check your work — use count() and glimpse() frequently

In my next post, I’ll cover how to handle survey weights using the survey package in R.

title: Beautiful Statistical Graphics with ggplot2
text:
When I first switched from Excel to R for data visualization, ggplot2 felt confusing. “Why do I need aes()? What’s a geom?” After years of use, I now think it’s the most elegant visualization system ever designed.
The key insight is the Grammar of Graphics: every chart is built from composable layers.

title: Beautiful Statistical Graphics with ggplot2
text:
When I first switched from Excel to R for data visualization, ggplot2 felt confusing. “Why do I need aes()? What’s a geom?” After years of use, I now think it’s the most elegant visualization system ever designed.
The key insight is the Grammar of Graphics: every chart is built from composable layers.

title: Beautiful Statistical Graphics with ggplot2
text:
The Foundation: Data + Aesthetics + Geometry


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(scales)

# Create a realistic wage dataset
set.seed(2025)
wage_data <- data.frame(
  state     = rep(c("Haryana","Punjab","UP","Bihar","Maharashtra",
                    "Gujarat","Karnataka","Tamil Nadu"), each = 200),
  wage      = c(
    rnorm(200, 450, 80), rnorm(200, 480, 90), rnorm(200, 320, 70),
    rnorm(200, 280, 65), rnorm(200, 520, 110), rnorm(200, 490, 95),
    rnorm(200, 510, 100), rnorm(200, 535, 105)
  ),
  gender    = sample(c("Male","Female"), 1600, replace = TRUE, prob=c(.6,.4)),
  sector    = sample(c("Agriculture","Industry","Services"), 1600, replace=TRUE)
)

title: Beautiful Statistical Graphics with ggplot2
text:
Layer 1: The Basic Plot


Code
# Start simple
ggplot(wage_data, aes(x = wage)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "#2563eb", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Wage Distribution", x = "Daily Wage (₹)", y = "Count")

title: Beautiful Statistical Graphics with ggplot2
text:
Layer 2: Add Facets and Color


Code
ggplot(wage_data, aes(x = wage, fill = gender)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.6, adjust = 1.2) +
  facet_wrap(~sector, ncol = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Male"="#2563eb","Female"="#e11d48")) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_number(prefix="₹")) +
  labs(title = "Wage Distribution by Sector and Gender",
       x = "Daily Wage (₹)", y = "Density", fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        strip.text = element_text(face="bold"),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

title: Beautiful Statistical Graphics with ggplot2
text:
Layer 3: Publication-Quality Box Plot


Code
state_summary <- wage_data |>
  group_by(state) |>
  summarise(median_wage = median(wage), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(median_wage)

wage_data |>
  mutate(state = factor(state, levels = state_summary$state)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = state, y = wage, fill = gender)) +
  geom_boxplot(
    outlier.shape = 21, outlier.size = 1, outlier.alpha = 0.4,
    width = 0.6, position = position_dodge(0.7), alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(group = gender),
    fun = mean, geom = "point",
    shape = 23, size = 3, fill = "white",
    position = position_dodge(0.7)
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Male"="#2563eb","Female"="#e11d48"),
                    name = "Gender") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_number(prefix = "₹"),
    breaks = seq(0, 800, 100)
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Daily Wage Distribution by State and Gender",
    subtitle = "Diamonds show group means · Boxes show IQR · Lines show medians",
    x = NULL, y = "Daily Wage (₹)",
    caption  = "Source: Simulated survey data for demonstration"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(color = "#64748b", size = 10),
    plot.caption     = element_text(color = "#94a3b8", size = 9),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    legend.position    = "top",
    plot.background    = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    plot.margin        = margin(16, 24, 12, 16)
  )





State-wise wage comparison — publication quality

title: Beautiful Statistical Graphics with ggplot2
text:
Layer 4: Custom Theme Function
The real power comes from creating your own reusable theme:


Code
theme_pawan <- function(base_size = 12) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      text              = element_text(family = "sans"),
      plot.title        = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size + 3, hjust = 0),
      plot.subtitle     = element_text(color = "#64748b", hjust = 0, margin = margin(b = 10)),
      plot.caption      = element_text(color = "#94a3b8", size = base_size - 3, hjust = 1),
      panel.grid.minor  = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major  = element_line(color = "#f1f5f9"),
      axis.title        = element_text(color = "#475569", size = base_size - 1),
      legend.position   = "top",
      legend.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
      plot.background   = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
      plot.margin       = margin(16, 24, 12, 16)
    )
}

# Use it
wage_data |>
  group_by(state, sector) |>
  summarise(mean_wage = mean(wage), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(state, mean_wage), y = mean_wage, fill = sector)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7, alpha = 0.9) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#2563eb","#7c3aed","#0d9488")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(prefix = "₹")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Mean Wage by State and Sector",
       subtitle = "Custom theme applied consistently across all charts",
       x = NULL, y = "Mean Daily Wage (₹)", fill = "Sector") +
  theme_pawan()









The theme_pawan() function means every chart in a report looks consistent — a crucial detail for professional work.

title: Sampling Methods in Field Research: What I Learned
text:
Textbooks make sampling sound clean. Reality is messier. In my years of field survey work — from rural Haryana to urban Maharashtra — I’ve encountered almost every challenge sampling theory describes, plus a few it doesn’t.
This post is my attempt to bridge the theory-practice gap.

title: Sampling Methods in Field Research: What I Learned
text:
Textbooks make sampling sound clean. Reality is messier. In my years of field survey work — from rural Haryana to urban Maharashtra — I’ve encountered almost every challenge sampling theory describes, plus a few it doesn’t.
This post is my attempt to bridge the theory-practice gap.

title: Sampling Methods in Field Research: What I Learned
section: Simple Random Sampling: Rarely Simple in Practice
text:
Simple Random Sampling: Rarely Simple in Practice


Code
library(tidyverse)

# Simulate a village population
set.seed(42)
village_pop <- data.frame(
  household_id = 1:500,
  income = exp(rnorm(500, log(8000), 0.8)),
  num_members = rpois(500, 4.5) + 1,
  landholding = rexp(500, 1/2.5)  # acres
)

# SRS: draw 50 households
srs_sample <- village_pop |>
  slice_sample(n = 50)

# Compare estimates to true values
comparison <- bind_rows(
  village_pop |> summarise(group = "Population", 
                           mean_income = mean(income),
                           mean_size = mean(num_members)),
  srs_sample |> summarise(group = "SRS Sample (n=50)", 
                           mean_income = mean(income),
                           mean_size = mean(num_members))
)

knitr::kable(comparison, digits = 1,
             caption = "SRS vs Population parameters")



SRS vs Population parameters


group
mean_income
mean_size




Population
10575.1
5.4


SRS Sample (n=50)
9324.0
5.3





The estimates are close — but in field reality, SRS requires a complete sampling frame (list of all households), which rarely exists in rural areas.

title: Sampling Methods in Field Research: What I Learned
text:
Stratified Sampling: My Preferred Approach
When I joined SSER, we almost always used stratified sampling for district-level surveys. Here’s why it works:


Code
# Add strata based on landholding
village_pop <- village_pop |>
  mutate(
    stratum = case_when(
      landholding == 0         ~ "Landless",
      landholding < 1          ~ "Marginal (<1 ac)",
      landholding < 5          ~ "Small (1-5 ac)",
      TRUE                     ~ "Large (5+ ac)"
    ) |> factor(levels = c("Landless","Marginal (<1 ac)","Small (1-5 ac)","Large (5+ ac)"))
  )

# Proportional stratified sample
strat_sample <- village_pop |>
  group_by(stratum) |>
  slice_sample(prop = 0.1) |>  # 10% from each stratum
  ungroup()

# Visualise income by stratum
ggplot(village_pop, aes(x = stratum, y = income, fill = stratum)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.shape = 21) +
  geom_jitter(data = strat_sample, aes(x = stratum, y = income),
              width = 0.2, alpha = 0.6, color = "black", size = 1.5) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::label_number(prefix = "₹", big.mark = ",")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#2563eb","#7c3aed","#0d9488","#d97706")) +
  labs(title = "Income Distribution by Land Stratum",
       subtitle = "Black dots = sampled households (10% per stratum)",
       x = NULL, y = "Monthly Income (log scale)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1))

title: Sampling Methods in Field Research: What I Learned
text:
Cluster Sampling: Practical but Tricky
Cluster sampling is often the only feasible approach for large geographic surveys. We used it extensively when surveying across multiple districts.


Code
# Simulate 20 clusters (villages), sample 5 clusters
set.seed(99)
n_clusters <- 20
clusters <- data.frame(
  cluster_id = 1:n_clusters,
  district = sample(c("Rohtak","Hisar","Karnal","Panipat"), 
                    n_clusters, replace = TRUE),
  pop_size = sample(200:800, n_clusters)
)

# Within-cluster correlation (ICC) effect
icc <- 0.15  # typical for income surveys
design_effect <- 1 + (mean(clusters$pop_size) - 1) * icc

cat("Design Effect (DEFF):", round(design_effect, 2))


Design Effect (DEFF): 84.51


Code
cat("\nEffective sample size reduction:", round((1 - 1/design_effect)*100, 1), "%")



Effective sample size reduction: 98.8 %


The key lesson: Always account for the design effect when reporting standard errors from cluster samples. Ignoring it gives you artificially small standard errors and overconfident conclusions.

title: Sampling Methods in Field Research: What I Learned
text:
Practical Tips from the Field

Listing comes first — Before sampling, build your sampling frame carefully. In rural areas, this often means a fresh household listing exercise.
Replacement rules matter — Define substitution procedures before going to field. What happens when a sampled household is locked or refuses?
Random starts in systematic sampling — Always use a random start for systematic samples, not just household #1.
Weight your data — After collecting, compute and apply sampling weights before any analysis. The survey package in R handles this beautifully.

Field sampling is as much art as science. The best designs balance statistical efficiency with logistical feasibility.

title: Chapter 1: Descriptive Statistics
text: Learning Objectives: By the end of this chapter, you will be able to compute and interpret measures of central tendency and dispersion, create effective visualizations for exploring distributions, and identify skewness and outliers in real datasets.

title: Chapter 1: Descriptive Statistics
text:
1 Introduction to Descriptive Statistics
Descriptive statistics summarize and describe the main features of a dataset. They are the first step in any data analysis — before we run a single regression or hypothesis test, we must understand what our data looks like.
In R, we have powerful tools for this. Let’s load our packages and create a realistic dataset:


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)

# Simulate Labour Force Survey data
set.seed(2025)
n <- 1000

lfs_data <- data.frame(
  age       = round(rnorm(n, 35, 12)) |> pmax(15) |> pmin(70),
  wage      = exp(rnorm(n, log(400), 0.7)) |> round(),
  edu_years = sample(0:20, n, replace = TRUE, 
                     prob = dpois(0:20, 8) + 0.01),
  gender    = sample(c("Male","Female"), n, replace = TRUE, prob=c(.6,.4)),
  state     = sample(c("Haryana","UP","Bihar","Maharashtra","Karnataka"), 
                     n, replace = TRUE),
  sector    = sample(c("Agriculture","Industry","Services"), n, 
                     replace = TRUE, prob = c(.4,.2,.4))
)

glimpse(lfs_data)


Rows: 1,000
Columns: 6
$ age       <dbl> 42, 35, 44, 50, 39, 33, 40, 34, 31, 43, 30, 15, 30, 44, 48, …
$ wage      <dbl> 737, 93, 261, 148, 239, 242, 229, 505, 385, 337, 256, 148, 2…
$ edu_years <int> 2, 4, 4, 2, 9, 8, 16, 11, 5, 4, 7, 10, 4, 9, 10, 6, 10, 13, …
$ gender    <chr> "Male", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Femal…
$ state     <chr> "UP", "Maharashtra", "Haryana", "UP", "Karnataka", "Bihar", …
$ sector    <chr> "Services", "Agriculture", "Services", "Industry", "Industry…

title: Chapter 1: Descriptive Statistics
text:
2 Measures of Central Tendency
The three main measures of central tendency each tell a different story:


Code
wage_stats <- lfs_data |>
  summarise(
    Mean   = mean(wage),
    Median = median(wage),
    Mode   = as.numeric(names(sort(table(round(wage,-2)), decreasing=TRUE)[1])),
    SD     = sd(wage),
    IQR    = IQR(wage),
    Min    = min(wage),
    Max    = max(wage),
    Skewness = (mean(wage) - median(wage)) / sd(wage) * 3  # Pearson's approx
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Statistic", values_to = "Value") |>
  mutate(Value = round(Value, 2))

kable(wage_stats, caption = "Descriptive Statistics for Daily Wage (₹)")



Descriptive Statistics for Daily Wage (₹)


Statistic
Value




Mean
505.76


Median
394.00


Mode
200.00


SD
444.35


IQR
397.25


Min
46.00


Max
6001.00


Skewness
0.75






2.1 Why the Mean ≠ Median for Wages


Code
ggplot(lfs_data, aes(x = wage)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "#93c5fd", alpha = 0.8, color = "white") +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = mean(wage), linetype = "Mean"), 
             color = "#2563eb", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = median(wage), linetype = "Median"), 
             color = "#e11d48", linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_linetype_manual(name = "", values = c("Mean"="solid","Median"="dashed")) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(prefix = "₹")) +
  labs(title = "Daily Wage Distribution",
       subtitle = "Right skew pulls the mean above the median",
       x = "Daily Wage (₹)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())





Wage distribution showing mean vs median divergence due to right skew




Key insight: For right-skewed income distributions, the median is almost always a better measure of “typical” than the mean. A few high earners inflate the mean.

4.2 Detecting Outliers


Code
# IQR method for outlier detection
wage_bounds <- lfs_data |>
  summarise(
    Q1  = quantile(wage, 0.25),
    Q3  = quantile(wage, 0.75),
    IQR = IQR(wage),
    lower = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR,
    upper = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
  )

lfs_outliers <- lfs_data |>
  mutate(is_outlier = wage < wage_bounds$lower | wage > wage_bounds$upper)

cat("Outliers detected (IQR method):", sum(lfs_outliers$is_outlier), "\n")

title: Chapter 3: Regression Analysis
text: Learning Objectives: Fit and interpret simple and multiple linear regression models, check OLS assumptions through diagnostic plots, and communicate results clearly.

title: Chapter 3: Regression Analysis
text:
1 What is Regression?
Regression analysis quantifies the relationship between variables. The goal is to model how changes in explanatory variables (X) are associated with changes in the outcome variable (Y).
The linear regression model: \(Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \cdots + \beta_k X_{ki} + \varepsilon_i\)


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(broom)

# Wage dataset with labour economics flavor
set.seed(2025)
n <- 800
wage_data <- data.frame(
  edu_years = sample(0:20, n, replace=TRUE, prob=dpois(0:20, 9)+0.01),
  experience = sample(0:40, n, replace=TRUE),
  female = rbinom(n, 1, 0.4),
  urban = rbinom(n, 1, 0.55),
  union = rbinom(n, 1, 0.18)
) |>
  mutate(
    log_wage = 4.5 + 0.08*edu_years + 0.04*experience - 0.003*experience^2
               - 0.25*female + 0.15*urban + 0.12*union + rnorm(n, 0, 0.35),
    wage = round(exp(log_wage))
  )

title: Chapter 3: Regression Analysis
text:
2 Simple Linear Regression
Let’s first explore the education-wage relationship:


Code
# Scatter with regression line
ggplot(wage_data, aes(x=edu_years, y=wage)) +
  geom_point(alpha=0.3, color="#2563eb", size=1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method="lm", color="#e11d48", se=TRUE, linewidth=1.2,
              fill="#fca5a5", alpha=0.3) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::label_number(prefix="₹")) +
  labs(title="Years of Education vs Daily Wage",
       subtitle="OLS regression line with 95% confidence band",
       x="Years of Education", y="Daily Wage (₹)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor=element_blank())











Code
# Fit simple linear regression
model_simple <- lm(wage ~ edu_years, data=wage_data)
tidy(model_simple) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), round, 3)) |>
  kable(caption="Simple Linear Regression: Wage ~ Education")

title: Chapter 3: Regression Analysis
text:
3 Multiple Linear Regression
Now let’s add more variables:


Code
model_full <- lm(wage ~ edu_years + experience + I(experience^2) + 
                   female + urban + union, data=wage_data)

tidy(model_full) |>
  mutate(
    term = recode(term,
      `(Intercept)`="Intercept",
      edu_years="Education (years)",
      experience="Experience (years)",
      `I(experience^2)`="Experience² (quadratic)",
      female="Female (=1)",
      urban="Urban (=1)",
      union="Union member (=1)"
    ),
    across(where(is.numeric), round, 4),
    significance = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "***",
      p.value < 0.01  ~ "**",
      p.value < 0.05  ~ "*",
      TRUE            ~ ""
    )
  ) |>
  kable(caption="Multiple Regression Results (*** p<0.001, ** p<0.01, * p<0.05)")

title: Chapter 3: Regression Analysis
text:
4 Regression Diagnostics
Always check the four core OLS assumptions:


Code
par(mfrow=c(2,2), mar=c(4,4,3,1), family="sans")
plot(model_full, which=1:4, 
     col=alpha("#2563eb", 0.4), pch=20, cex=0.7,
     col.smooth="#e11d48", lwd.smooth=2)





Regression diagnostic plots — checking OLS assumptions




Code
par(mfrow=c(1,1))


What to look for: - Residuals vs Fitted: Should show no pattern (random scatter around 0) - Q-Q Plot: Points should follow the diagonal line (normality of residuals) - Scale-Location: Horizontal band (homoscedasticity — equal variance) - Cook’s Distance: Points above 0.5 are influential observations

title: Chapter 3: Regression Analysis
text:
5 Coefficient Plot


Code
tidy(model_full, conf.int=TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(term = recode(term,
    edu_years="Education", experience="Experience",
    `I(experience^2)`="Experience²",
    female="Female", urban="Urban", union="Union")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x=estimate, y=reorder(term, estimate))) +
  geom_vline(xintercept=0, linetype="dashed", color="#94a3b8") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin=conf.low, xmax=conf.high), height=0.25,
                 color="#7c3aed", linewidth=0.8) +
  geom_point(size=4, color="#2563eb") +
  labs(title="Regression Coefficients with 95% CI",
       x="Coefficient Estimate (₹/unit change)", y=NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor=element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.y=element_blank())

title: Chapter 3: Regression Analysis
text:
6 Log-Linear Wage Equation (Mincer Model)
A standard approach in labour economics — using log wages gives percentage interpretations:


Code
model_log <- lm(log_wage ~ edu_years + experience + I(experience^2) + 
                  female + urban + union, data=wage_data)

tidy(model_log) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(
    term = recode(term,
      edu_years="Education (years)",
      experience="Experience",
      `I(experience^2)`="Experience²",
      female="Female (=1)",
      urban="Urban (=1)",
      union="Union (=1)"),
    pct_effect = round((exp(estimate)-1)*100, 2),
    across(c(estimate, std.error, p.value), round, 4)
  ) |>
  select(term, estimate, std.error, p.value, pct_effect) |>
  rename(`% Effect` = pct_effect) |>
  kable(caption="Mincer Wage Equation (Log-Linear): % change in wage per unit")

title: Data Visualizations
text:
Explore the relationship between GDP per capita and life expectancy across countries. Use the controls to filter and zoom.


Code
set.seed(42)
n <- 150
df <- data.frame(
  country   = paste("Country", 1:n),
  gdp_pc    = exp(rnorm(n, 9, 1.2)),
  life_exp  = 50 + 25 * (1 - exp(-exp(rnorm(n, 9, 1.2)) / 30000)) + rnorm(n, 0, 2),
  population = exp(rnorm(n, 15, 2)),
  region    = sample(c("Asia","Europe","Africa","Americas","Oceania"), n, replace = TRUE)
)

fig <- plot_ly(
  df, x = ~gdp_pc, y = ~life_exp,
  size = ~population,
  color = ~region,
  colors = pal,
  text = ~paste0("<b>", country, "</b><br>",
                 "GDP/capita: $", round(gdp_pc), "<br>",
                 "Life Expectancy: ", round(life_exp, 1), " yrs<br>",
                 "Population: ", format(round(population), big.mark = ",")),
  hoverinfo = "text",
  type = "scatter", mode = "markers",
  marker = list(opacity = 0.75, sizemode = "area", sizeref = 2.0 * max(df$population) / (40^2))
) |>
  layout(
    title = list(text = "GDP per Capita vs Life Expectancy", font = list(size = 16)),
    xaxis = list(title = "GDP per Capita (log scale)", type = "log",
                 gridcolor = "#f1f5f9"),
    yaxis = list(title = "Life Expectancy (years)", gridcolor = "#f1f5f9"),
    plot_bgcolor = "white",
    paper_bgcolor = "white",
    legend = list(title = list(text = "Region")),
    hoverlabel = list(bgcolor = "#1e293b", font = list(color = "white"))
  )
fig

title: Data Visualizations
text:
2 · Animated Time-Series
Watch how employment across sectors evolved from 2015 to 2024.


Code
years <- 2015:2024
sectors <- c("Agriculture","Industry","Services","Technology","Healthcare")

set.seed(7)
ts_df <- expand.grid(Year = years, Sector = sectors) |>
  arrange(Sector, Year) |>
  group_by(Sector) |>
  mutate(
    base   = c(Agriculture = 45, Industry = 25, Services = 38, Technology = 12, Healthcare = 18)[Sector],
    trend  = c(Agriculture = -0.8, Industry = 0.3, Services = 1.2, Technology = 2.5, Healthcare = 1.8)[Sector],
    Employment = base + trend * (Year - 2015) + cumsum(rnorm(n(), 0, 0.5))
  ) |> ungroup()

fig2 <- plot_ly(ts_df, x = ~Year, y = ~Employment,
                color = ~Sector, colors = pal,
                frame = ~Year,
                type = "scatter", mode = "lines+markers",
                line = list(width = 3),
                marker = list(size = 8)) |>
  layout(
    title  = "Employment Share by Sector (2015–2024)",
    xaxis  = list(title = "Year", gridcolor = "#f1f5f9"),
    yaxis  = list(title = "Employment Share (%)", gridcolor = "#f1f5f9"),
    plot_bgcolor  = "white",
    paper_bgcolor = "white"
  ) |>
  animation_opts(frame = 600, easing = "cubic-in-out", redraw = FALSE) |>
  animation_slider(currentvalue = list(prefix = "Year: "))

fig2

title: Data Visualizations
text:
5 · Correlation Heatmap


Code
set.seed(99)
vars <- c("Income","Education","Age","Health Score","Employment\nDuration",
          "Savings Rate","Work Hours","Satisfaction")
n_vars <- length(vars)
cor_mat <- matrix(runif(n_vars^2, -1, 1), n_vars, n_vars)
cor_mat <- (cor_mat + t(cor_mat)) / 2
diag(cor_mat) <- 1
cor_mat <- round(cor_mat, 2)
rownames(cor_mat) <- colnames(cor_mat) <- vars

fig5 <- plot_ly(
  z = cor_mat,
  x = vars, y = vars,
  type = "heatmap",
  colorscale = list(c(0,"#e11d48"), c(0.5,"white"), c(1,"#2563eb")),
  zmin = -1, zmax = 1,
  text = cor_mat,
  hovertemplate = "%{y} × %{x}<br>r = %{z}<extra></extra>"
) |>
  layout(
    title = "Variable Correlation Matrix",
    xaxis = list(tickangle = -30),
    plot_bgcolor  = "white",
    paper_bgcolor = "white"
  )
fig5

title: Data Visualizations
section: 6 · Labour Force Survey — Static Publication-Quality Chart
text:
6 · Labour Force Survey — Static Publication-Quality Chart


Code
set.seed(2025)
states <- c("Haryana","Punjab","UP","Bihar","Maharashtra","Gujarat",
            "Karnataka","Tamil Nadu","Rajasthan","MP","WB","Odisha")

plfs_df <- data.frame(
  state = rep(states, each = 2),
  gender = rep(c("Male","Female"), length(states)),
  wpr = c(
    75, 28, 78, 22, 64, 18, 62, 15, 80, 30, 82, 35,
    76, 32, 74, 38, 70, 24, 66, 20, 72, 26, 68, 22
  )
)

ggplot(plfs_df, aes(x = reorder(state, wpr), y = wpr, fill = gender)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.65, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(wpr, "%")),
            position = position_dodge(0.65),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 2.8, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Male" = "#2563eb", "Female" = "#e11d48"),
                    name = "Gender") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 100), labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Worker Participation Rate by State",
    subtitle = "Simulated Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS) style data, 2024",
    x = NULL, y = "Worker Participation Rate (%)",
    caption  = "Source: Simulated data for demonstration purposes"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14, hjust = 0),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(color = "#64748b", hjust = 0),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    legend.position    = "top",
    plot.background    = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    plot.margin        = margin(16, 24, 16, 16)
  )





Worker Participation Rate by State (Simulated PLFS-style data)

title: India’s Fertilizer Import Landscape: Supply Concentration & Price Dynamics
text: India is the world’s second-largest consumer of fertilizers. With domestic production insufficient to meet demand for nitrogenous (Urea), phosphatic (DAP, Rock Phosphate, Phosphoric Acid), and potassic (MOP) nutrients, India imports substantial quantities each year. This analysis examines FY 2016–2024 import trends, source-country concentration, international price volatility, and domestic Urea/DAP production.

title: India’s Fertilizer Import Landscape: Supply Concentration & Price Dynamics
text:
Setup


Code
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggiraph)
library(scales)
library(gt)
library(viridis)
library(plotly)
library(tidyr)
library(patchwork)
options(scipen = 99999)

my_theme <- theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, margin = margin(b = 8)),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5, color = "#7F8C8D", margin = margin(b = 10)),
    plot.caption    = element_text(size = 9, color = "#95A5A6", hjust = 0),
    axis.title      = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    axis.text       = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title    = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major= element_line(color = "#ECF0F1", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor= element_blank(),
    strip.background= element_rect(fill = "#F8F9FA", color = NA),
    strip.text      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11)
  )

## Interactive stacked bar helper
make_fert_girafe <- function(data, title, subtitle, pal = NULL) {
  data <- as.data.table(data)
  data[, tt := paste0("<b>", Country, "</b><br>Year: ", Year,
                      "<br>Qty: ", round(Qty,2), " MT<br>Share: ", Percent, "%")]
  p <- ggplot(data, aes(x = Year, y = Qty, fill = Country)) +
    geom_bar_interactive(aes(tooltip = tt, data_id = Country),
                         stat = "identity", position = "stack", width = 0.75, alpha = 0.9) +
    geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(Percent < 9, "", paste0(Percent, "%"))),
              position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
              size = 3, color = "white", fontface = "bold") +
    my_theme +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = comma_format()) +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Qty (Million Tonnes)", title = title, subtitle = subtitle,
         caption = "Source: Ministry of Commerce, India | Analysis: Pawan")
  if (!is.null(pal)) p <- p + scale_fill_manual(values = pal)
  else               p <- p + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2")
  girafe(ggobj = p, width_svg = 11, height_svg = 6,
         options = list(
           opts_tooltip(css = "background:#fff;color:#333;padding:8px;border-radius:5px;border:1px solid #ddd;font-size:12px;"),
           opts_hover(css = "opacity:0.85;stroke:#333;stroke-width:1.5;"),
           opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.3;"),
           opts_zoom(min = 0.8, max = 3),
           opts_sizing(rescale = TRUE, width = 1)))
}

## Data loading & cleaning helper
load_fert <- function(path) {
  dt <- readRDS(path)
  dt$ImportMonth <- gsub(",","",dt$ImportMonth)
  dt
}

prep_country <- function(dt, keep_countries, factor_levels) {
  dt <- dt[Country != "Total",
           .(Qty = sum(as.numeric(ImportMonth), na.rm=TRUE)), .(Country, Year)]
  dt[!(Country %in% keep_countries), Country := "OTHER COUNTRY"]
  dt <- dt[, .(Qty = sum(Qty)), .(Country, Year)]
  dt[, Qty := Qty/100000]
  dt <- dt[Year > "2015", .(Qty = sum(Qty)), .(Country, Year)]
  dt[, Percent := round(Qty*100/sum(Qty)), by = Year]
  dt[, Country := factor(Country, levels = factor_levels)]
  dt
}

title: India’s Fertilizer Import Landscape: Supply Concentration & Price Dynamics
text:
Section 1 · Urea Imports
Urea is India’s most consumed fertilizer. Despite domestic production, India imports 6–10 million tonnes annually. China, Iran, and Oman dominate supply; Russia has emerged as a key source in recent years.


Code
raw_urea <- load_fert("/home/pawan/fertilizer/urea_importqty.rds")
raw_urea[Country == "CHINA P RP", Country := "CHINA"]
raw_urea[Country == "EGYPT A RP", Country := "EGYPT"]

t_urea <- prep_country(
  raw_urea,
  keep_countries = c("CHINA","OMAN","IRAN","UKRAINE","EGYPT","RUSSIA","INDONESIA"),
  factor_levels  = c("OTHER COUNTRY","INDONESIA","EGYPT","RUSSIA","UKRAINE","OMAN","IRAN","CHINA")
)

make_fert_girafe(t_urea,
  title    = "India's Urea Imports by Source Country",
  subtitle = "China, Iran & Oman historically dominant; Russia emerging")

title: India’s Fertilizer Import Landscape: Supply Concentration & Price Dynamics
text:
Section 2 · DAP Imports
Di-ammonium phosphate (DAP) is the primary phosphatic fertilizer in India. China and Saudi Arabia have historically led supply; Morocco and Russia play secondary roles.


Code
raw_dap <- load_fert("/home/pawan/fertilizer/dap_importqty.rds")
raw_dap[Country == "CHINA P RP", Country := "CHINA"]

t_dap <- prep_country(
  raw_dap,
  keep_countries = c("CHINA","SAUDI ARAB","MOROCCO","JORDAN","RUSSIA"),
  factor_levels  = c("OTHER COUNTRY","MOROCCO","JORDAN","RUSSIA","CHINA","SAUDI ARAB")
)

make_fert_girafe(t_dap,
  title    = "India's DAP Imports by Source Country",
  subtitle = "Saudi Arabia & China lead; Morocco & Russia provide diversification")

title: India’s Fertilizer Import Landscape: Supply Concentration & Price Dynamics
text:
Section 3 · Rock Phosphate Imports
Rock phosphate is the upstream input for phosphoric acid and superphosphates. Jordan and Morocco — both in the phosphate-rich North Africa/Middle East belt — dominate India’s supply.


Code
raw_rp <- load_fert("/home/pawan/fertilizer/rock_phosphate_importqty.rds")
raw_rp[Country == "EGYPT A RP", Country := "EGYPT"]

t_rp <- prep_country(
  raw_rp,
  keep_countries = c("EGYPT","JORDAN","MOROCCO","PERU","TOGO"),
  factor_levels  = c("OTHER COUNTRY","PERU","TOGO","EGYPT","MOROCCO","JORDAN")
)

make_fert_girafe(t_rp,
  title    = "India's Rock Phosphate Imports by Source Country",
  subtitle = "Jordan & Morocco dominate; geographic concentration is a risk")

title: India’s Fertilizer Import Landscape: Supply Concentration & Price Dynamics
text:
Section 4 · Phosphoric Acid Imports


Code
raw_pa <- load_fert("/home/pawan/fertilizer/phosphoric_acid_importqty.rds")

t_pa <- prep_country(
  raw_pa,
  keep_countries = c("MOROCCO","JORDAN","SENEGAL"),
  factor_levels  = c("OTHER COUNTRY","SENEGAL","JORDAN","MOROCCO")
)

make_fert_girafe(t_pa,
  title    = "India's Phosphoric Acid Imports by Source Country",
  subtitle = "Morocco leads; Senegal and Jordan secondary suppliers")

title: India’s Fertilizer Import Landscape: Supply Concentration & Price Dynamics
text:
Section 5 · MOP (Muriate of Potash) Imports
India has no domestic potash resources and imports 100% of its MOP needs. Belarus and Canada have historically led, with Russia and Jordan filling gaps.


Code
raw_mop <- load_fert("/home/pawan/fertilizer/potash_importqty.rds")

t_mop <- prep_country(
  raw_mop,
  keep_countries = c("CANADA","BELARUS","ISRAEL","JORDAN","LITHUANIA","RUSSIA"),
  factor_levels  = c("OTHER COUNTRY","RUSSIA","LITHUANIA","JORDAN","ISRAEL","BELARUS","CANADA")
)

make_fert_girafe(t_mop,
  title    = "India's MOP Imports by Source Country",
  subtitle = "Canada & Belarus historically dominant; Russia share rising post-2022")

title: India’s Fertilizer Import Landscape: Supply Concentration & Price Dynamics
text:
Section 6 · International Fertilizer Prices
Global fertilizer prices are a major driver of India’s import bill and subsidy expenditure. The 2021–22 price spike — driven by energy costs, sanctions, and supply disruptions — dramatically increased India’s fertilizer subsidy burden.


Code
price <- fread("/home/pawan/fertilizer/international_fert_price.csv")
price[, Year := paste0("20", Year)]
price[, Month := substr(Month, 1, 3)]
price[, Date := as.Date(paste(Year, Month, 20, sep="-"), format="%Y-%b-%d")]

price_long <- melt(price, id.vars = c("Month","Year","Date"))
price_long <- price_long[variable %in% c("Urea","DAP","MOP")]

## Plotly line chart — richer interactivity
p_price <- ggplot(price_long, aes(x=Date, y=value, color=variable,
                                   text=paste0(variable,"\n",format(Date,"%b %Y"),
                                               "\n$",round(value)," /MT"))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_smooth(method="loess", se=FALSE, linewidth=0.6, linetype="dashed", alpha=0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~variable, scales="free_y") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette="Set1") +
  my_theme + theme(legend.position="none") +
  scale_x_date(date_labels="%b '%y", date_breaks="6 months") +
  labs(title="International Fertilizer Prices (USD/MT)",
       subtitle="2021–22 price shock visible in all three fertilizers",
       y="Price (USD/MT)", x=NULL,
       caption="Source: World Bank / FAO | Analysis: Pawan")

ggplotly(p_price, tooltip="text") |>
  layout(hovermode="x unified")







Year-on-Year Price Change


Code
price_annual <- price_long[, .(Avg_Price = mean(value, na.rm=TRUE)), .(Year, variable)]
setorder(price_annual, variable, Year)
price_annual[, YoY := round((Avg_Price/shift(Avg_Price)-1)*100, 1), by=variable]

gt(dcast(price_annual, Year ~ variable, value.var="Avg_Price")) |>
  tab_header(title="Annual Average International Fertilizer Price (USD/MT)") |>
  fmt_number(columns=2:4, decimals=0) |>
  data_color(columns=c("Urea","DAP","MOP"),
             colors=scales::col_numeric(c("#D5F5E3","#FDFEFE","#FADBD8"), domain=NULL)) |>

  tab_source_note("Source: World Bank Commodity Prices")

title: India’s Fertilizer Import Landscape: Supply Concentration & Price Dynamics
text:
Section 7 · Domestic Urea & DAP Production


Code
fert <- readRDS("/home/pawan/fertilizer/production-import/fert_production_import.RDS")
fert <- fert[Year != "Total"]
fert[, Date := as.Date(paste(Year,"-",Month,"-",15,sep=""), "%Y-%b-%d")]
fert_long <- melt(fert, id.vars=c("Year","Month","Date"))
fert_long <- fert_long[variable!="SNo" & variable!="Blank"]
fert_long <- separate(fert_long, variable, into=c("Fertilizer","Variable"), sep="_")
setDT(fert_long)
prod_old <- fert_long[Variable=="Production"]
prod_old[, value := as.numeric(value)/100000]
prod_old[, Year  := as.integer(Year)]

fert1 <- fread("/home/pawan/fertilizer/production-import.csv")
fert1[, Date := as.Date(paste(Year,"-",Month,"-",15,sep=""), "%Y-%b-%d")]
fert1_long <- melt(fert1, id.vars=c("Year","Month","Date","Fertilizer"))
fert1_long[variable == "Production_Achievement", variable := "Production"]
prod_new <- fert1_long[variable == "Production"]
prod_new[, Year := as.integer(Year)]

prod_all <- rbind(
  prod_old[Year < 2018, .(Year,Month,Date,Fertilizer,value)],
  prod_new[, .(Year,Month,Date,Fertilizer,value)]
)
prod_all <- prod_all[Fertilizer %in% c("Urea","DAP") & !is.na(value) & Year != 2025]
prod_annual <- prod_all[, .(Qty = sum(value, na.rm=TRUE)), .(Year, Fertilizer)]

## Area + line combo
p_prod <- ggplot(prod_annual, aes(x=Year, y=Qty, color=Fertilizer, fill=Fertilizer,
                                   text=paste0(Fertilizer," (",Year,"): ",round(Qty,2)," MT"))) +
  geom_area(alpha=0.2, position="identity") +
  geom_line(linewidth=1.3) +
  geom_point(size=2.5) +
  facet_wrap(~Fertilizer, scales="free_y", ncol=1) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("Urea"="#3498DB","DAP"="#E67E22")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("Urea"="#3498DB","DAP"="#E67E22")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks=seq(1990,2025,2)) +
  my_theme + theme(legend.position="none") +
  labs(title="Domestic Urea & DAP Production in India",
       subtitle="Urea capacity near self-sufficient; DAP heavily import-dependent",
       y="Million Tonnes", x=NULL,
       caption="Source: Fertilizer Association of India | Analysis: Pawan")

ggplotly(p_prod, tooltip="text") |> layout(hovermode="x unified")

title: India’s Fertilizer Import Landscape: Supply Concentration & Price Dynamics

Section 8 · Supply Concentration Risk Summary

risk_data <- data.table(
  Fertilizer = c("Urea","DAP","Rock Phosphate","Phosphoric Acid","MOP"),
  Top_Supplier_1 = c("China","Saudi Arabia","Jordan","Morocco","Canada"),
  Top_Supplier_2 = c("Iran","China","Morocco","Jordan","Belarus"),
  Top2_Share_Pct = c(65, 72, 80, 85, 70),

                                       levels=c("Low","Medium","High","Very High"))) |>

title: Fuel Price Analysis: India’s Retail Prices & International Crude Dynamics
text: India imports ~85% of its crude oil needs, making domestic fuel prices highly sensitive to global energy markets. This analysis tracks the Indian Basket crude price, international petrol/diesel FOB prices, retail prices in four metro cities, and aggregate petroleum import volumes. Key events — COVID-19 demand collapse (2020), the 2022 Russia–Ukraine war price spike, and periodic government tax adjustments — are visible in the data.

title: Fuel Price Analysis: India’s Retail Prices & International Crude Dynamics
text:
Setup


Code
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggiraph)
library(scales)
library(plotly)
library(readxl)
library(lubridate)
library(patchwork)
library(gt)

my_theme <- theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, margin = margin(b = 8)),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5, color = "#7F8C8D"),
    plot.caption    = element_text(size = 9, color = "#95A5A6", hjust = 0),
    axis.title      = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    axis.text       = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title    = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major= element_line(color = "#ECF0F1", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor= element_blank(),
    strip.background= element_rect(fill = "#F8F9FA", color = NA),
    strip.text      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11)
  )

title: Fuel Price Analysis: India’s Retail Prices & International Crude Dynamics
section: Section 1 · International Crude Oil Price (Indian Basket)
text:
Section 1 · International Crude Oil Price (Indian Basket)
The Indian Basket is a weighted average of Oman, Dubai (sour crude) and Brent (sweet crude). It is the reference price used for setting domestic fuel prices.


Code
crude_path <- "/home/pawan/fuelprices/international_price_indian_basket.xlsx"

crude <- as.data.table(read_excel(crude_path, sheet="crude-oil"))
crude_long <- melt(crude, id=c("Year","Average","Ratio"))
crude_long[, Month_num := as.integer(match(substr(variable,1,3), month.abb))]
crude_long[, FY_Year   := as.integer(ifelse(Month_num > 3,
                             substr(Year,1,4), paste0("20",substr(Year,6,7))))]
crude_long[, Date := as.Date(paste0(FY_Year,"-",Month_num,"-20"))]
crude_long <- crude_long[!is.na(value) & !is.na(Date)]

avg_crude <- crude_long[, .(Price = mean(value, na.rm=TRUE)), by=FY_Year]

## Interactive monthly chart
p_crude_m <- ggplot(crude_long, aes(x=Date, y=value, group=1,
                                     text=paste0(format(Date,"%b %Y"),"\n$",round(value,1),"/bbl"))) +
  geom_line(color="#2C3E50", linewidth=0.8) +
  geom_smooth(method="loess", se=TRUE, color="#E74C3C", fill="#FADBD8", linewidth=1, alpha=0.3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept=as.Date("2020-03-01"), linetype="dashed", color="#3498DB", alpha=0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept=as.Date("2022-02-24"), linetype="dashed", color="#E74C3C", alpha=0.7) +
  annotate("text", x=as.Date("2020-05-01"), y=max(crude_long$value,na.rm=TRUE)*0.9,
           label="COVID-19", color="#3498DB", size=3, hjust=0) +
  annotate("text", x=as.Date("2022-05-01"), y=max(crude_long$value,na.rm=TRUE)*0.95,
           label="Ukraine War", color="#E74C3C", size=3, hjust=0) +
  my_theme +
  scale_x_date(date_labels="%Y", date_breaks="2 years") +
  labs(title="Indian Basket Crude Oil Price (Monthly)",
       subtitle="Red shading = LOESS confidence interval | Key events annotated",
       y="USD per Barrel", x=NULL,
       caption="Source: PPAC, Ministry of Petroleum & Natural Gas")

ggplotly(p_crude_m, tooltip="text") |> layout(hovermode="x unified")








Code
p_crude_a <- ggplot(avg_crude, aes(x=FY_Year, y=Price,
                                    text=paste0("FY",FY_Year,"\nAvg: $",round(Price,1),"/bbl"))) +
  geom_col_interactive(aes(tooltip=paste0("FY",FY_Year,"\n$",round(Price,1),"/bbl"),
                             data_id=FY_Year),
                        fill="#2C3E50", alpha=0.8, width=0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label=paste0("$",round(Price))), vjust=-0.4, size=3.5, fontface="bold") +
  my_theme +
  labs(title="Annual Average Indian Basket Crude Price",
       y="USD per Barrel", x="Financial Year",
       caption="Source: PPAC | Analysis: Pawan")

girafe(ggobj=p_crude_a, width_svg=11, height_svg=5,
       options=list(opts_tooltip(css="background:#fff;color:#333;padding:8px;border-radius:5px;border:1px solid #ddd;"),
                    opts_hover(css="opacity:0.85;"),
                    opts_sizing(rescale=TRUE,width=1)))

title: Fuel Price Analysis: India’s Retail Prices & International Crude Dynamics
text:
Section 2 · International Petrol & Diesel Prices


Code
diesel <- as.data.table(read_excel(crude_path, sheet="diesel"))
petrol <- as.data.table(read_excel(crude_path, sheet="petrol"))

prep_fuel <- function(dt, fuel_name) {
  dt_long <- melt(dt, id=c("Year","Average"))
  dt_long[, Month_num := as.integer(match(substr(variable,1,3), month.abb))]
  dt_long[, FY_Year   := as.integer(ifelse(Month_num > 3,
                             substr(Year,1,4), paste0("20",substr(Year,6,7))))]
  dt_long[, Date := as.Date(paste0(FY_Year,"-",Month_num,"-20"))]
  dt_long[, Fuel := fuel_name]
  dt_long[!is.na(value) & !is.na(Date)]
}

diesel_l <- prep_fuel(diesel, "Diesel")
petrol_l <- prep_fuel(petrol, "Petrol")
fuel_intl <- rbind(diesel_l, petrol_l)

p_fuel <- ggplot(fuel_intl, aes(x=Date, y=value, color=Fuel,
                                 text=paste0(Fuel,"\n",format(Date,"%b %Y"),
                                             "\n$",round(value,1),"/bbl"))) +
  geom_line(linewidth=1) +
  facet_wrap(~Fuel) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("Diesel"="#3498DB","Petrol"="#E74C3C")) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels="%Y", date_breaks="2 years") +
  my_theme + theme(legend.position="none") +
  labs(title="International Diesel & Petrol FOB Prices",
       subtitle="Prices track crude with a small refining-margin premium",
       y="USD per Barrel", x=NULL,
       caption="Source: PPAC, Ministry of Petroleum & Natural Gas")

ggplotly(p_fuel, tooltip="text") |> layout(hovermode="x unified")

title: Fuel Price Analysis: India’s Retail Prices & International Crude Dynamics
text:
Section 3 · Petroleum Imports (Volume)


Code
imp_raw <- as.data.table(read_excel("/home/pawan/fuelprices/IMPORT_EXPORT.xlsx",
                                     sheet="PT_IMPORT_H"))
imp_long <- melt(imp_raw, id="IMPORT_EXPORT")
imp_sub  <- imp_long[IMPORT_EXPORT %in% c("CRUDE OIL","TOTAL PRODUCT IMPORT")]
imp_sub[, tt := paste0(IMPORT_EXPORT,"\nYear: ",variable,
                        "\nQty: ",format(round(value/1000,0), big.mark=",")," MMT")]

p_imp <- ggplot(imp_sub, aes(x=variable, y=value/1000, fill=IMPORT_EXPORT)) +
  geom_bar_interactive(aes(tooltip=tt, data_id=IMPORT_EXPORT),
                        stat="identity", position="stack", width=0.75, alpha=0.9) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("CRUDE OIL"="#2C3E50","TOTAL PRODUCT IMPORT"="#E67E22")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=comma_format()) +
  my_theme +
  labs(title="India's Petroleum Import Volumes",
       subtitle="Crude oil + refined product imports (MMT = million metric tonnes)",
       y="Million Metric Tonnes", x="Year",
       caption="Source: PPAC | Analysis: Pawan")

girafe(ggobj=p_imp, width_svg=12, height_svg=6,

## Merge monthly crude with Delhi retail prices (as proxy)

title: India’s Public Foodgrain Procurement: Trends, Patterns & Policy Implications
text:
India operates one of the world’s largest public food procurement systems. The government procures wheat (rabi), paddy/rice (kharif), and coarse grains at the Minimum Support Price (MSP) through the Food Corporation of India (FCI) and state government agencies. Procured stocks underpin the Public Distribution System (PDS), midday-meal schemes, and the National Food Security Act (NFSA) welfare programmes.
Key policy features: - Procurement is price-floor insurance for farmers — FCI and state agencies buy all offered quantities at MSP. - Stocks feed welfare: NFSA covers 80 crore beneficiaries with 5 kg/person/month of subsidised grain. - Buffer norms guide minimum stock requirements for food security.

title: India’s Public Foodgrain Procurement: Trends, Patterns & Policy Implications
text:
Setup


Code
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggiraph)
library(scales)

my_theme <- theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, margin = margin(b = 8)),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5, color = "#7F8C8D"),
    plot.caption    = element_text(size = 9, color = "#95A5A6", hjust = 0),
    axis.title      = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    axis.text       = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title    = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major= element_line(color = "#ECF0F1", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor= element_blank(),
    strip.background= element_rect(fill = "#F8F9FA", color = NA),
    strip.text      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11)
  )

Section 1 · Total Procurement by Crop


Code
t1 <- fread("/home/pawan/procurement/procurement.csv")

## Summarise by crop and marketing season
proc_sum <- t1[, .(Total     = sum(Total, na.rm=TRUE),
                   FCI       = sum(FCI, na.rm=TRUE),
                   StateAgency = sum(StateAgency, na.rm=TRUE)),
               by = .(Crop, MarketingSeason)]

proc_sum[, FCI_Share   := round(FCI/Total*100, 1)]
proc_sum[, State_Share := round(StateAgency/Total*100, 1)]

## By-crop faceted bar

       subtitle="Hover over bars for FCI vs state agency breakdown",
       y="Quantity (Lakh Metric Tonnes)", x="Marketing Season",
       caption="Source: DFPD Procurement Portal | Analysis: Pawan")

section: Section 2 · FCI vs State Agency Split
text:
Section 2 · FCI vs State Agency Split
The procurement machinery involves two tiers: FCI (central agency) and state government agencies. Their relative roles have shifted over time — states have taken on more responsibility as part of decentralisation.


Code
proc_long <- melt(proc_sum[, .(Crop,MarketingSeason,FCI,StateAgency)],
                  id.vars=c("Crop","MarketingSeason"),
                  variable.name="Agency", value.name="Qty")
proc_long[, tt := paste0(Agency,"\nCrop: ",Crop,"\nSeason: ",MarketingSeason,
                          "\n",round(Qty,1)," LMT")]

p2 <- ggplot(proc_long, aes(x=MarketingSeason, y=Qty, fill=Agency)) +
  geom_bar_interactive(aes(tooltip=tt, data_id=Agency),
                        stat="identity", position="stack", width=0.75, alpha=0.9) +
  facet_wrap(~Crop, scales="free_y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("FCI"="#2C3E50","StateAgency"="#E67E22"),
                    labels=c("FCI"="FCI","StateAgency"="State Agencies")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=comma_format()) +
  my_theme +
  labs(title="FCI vs State Agency Procurement Split",
       subtitle="States increasingly driving procurement in newer seasons",
       y="Quantity (LMT)", x="Marketing Season",
       caption="Source: DFPD | Analysis: Pawan")

girafe(ggobj=p2, width_svg=13, height_svg=7,
       options=list(
         opts_tooltip(css="background:#fff;color:#333;padding:8px;border-radius:5px;border:1px solid #ddd;"),
         opts_hover(css="opacity:0.85;stroke-width:1.5;"),
         opts_sizing(rescale=TRUE, width=1)))

title: India’s Public Foodgrain Procurement: Trends, Patterns & Policy Implications
text:
Section 5 · Policy Context


Code
## Paddy procurement regional breakdown (if available)
if ("State" %in% names(t1)) {
  paddy_state <- t1[Crop=="Paddy",
    .(Total=sum(Total,na.rm=TRUE)), .(State, MarketingSeason)]
  top_states <- paddy_state[, .(Total=sum(Total)), by=State][order(-Total)][1:10, State]
  paddy_top  <- paddy_state[State %in% top_states]

  p_state <- ggplot(paddy_top, aes(x=MarketingSeason, y=Total, fill=State)) +
    geom_bar(stat="identity", position="stack", width=0.75, alpha=0.9) +
    scale_fill_viridis_d() +
    my_theme +
    labs(title="Paddy Procurement by Top States",
         y="Quantity (LMT)", x="Marketing Season",
         caption="Source: DFPD | Analysis: Pawan")
  ggplotly(p_state) |> layout(hovermode="x unified")
}







Key insights:

Paddy dominates procurement in volume terms, with Chhattisgarh, MP, Punjab and Haryana as the largest procuring states.
Wheat procurement fluctuates more sharply due to production variability in the Indo-Gangetic plain.
State agencies have grown as a share of total procurement, reflecting greater state-level operational capacity.
COVID-19 impact (2020–21): procurement held firm, underpinning the free-grain scheme (PMGKAY) for 80 crore beneficiaries.

title: India’s Foodgrain Buffer Stocks: Trends, Norms, and Policy
text:
India maintains one of the world’s largest publicly managed foodgrain buffer stock systems. The Food Corporation of India (FCI) and state agencies procure, store, and distribute wheat and rice to approximately 800 million beneficiaries under the National Food Security Act (NFSA). The central pool stock position — reported monthly by FCI — is a critical indicator of India’s food security preparedness, the government’s procurement performance, and the adequacy of storage infrastructure.







TipKey Findings at a Glance




📦 Total foodgrain stocks peaked at over 100 LMT (10 MT) in early 2025, driven by large unmilled paddy holdings
🌾 Wheat stocks show sharp seasonal cycles: declining through the year as distribution continues, then replenishing sharply after the April–June procurement season
🍚 Rice stocks have grown steadily from ~21 LMT (early 2023) to ~34 LMT (early 2025)
⚠️ Wheat stocks hit a near-decade low of ~7.5 LMT in April 2024, raising brief food security concerns before the 2024 rabi harvest
📊 Stocks consistently exceed buffer norms: India over-procures relative to minimum requirements, creating storage pressure and fiscal costs
🌾 Unmilled paddy (dhaan) holdings have grown as procurement has expanded from traditional rice-bowl states to eastern India

title: India’s Foodgrain Buffer Stocks: Trends, Norms, and Policy
text:
1 Setup


Code
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggiraph)
library(tidyr)
library(scales)
library(lubridate)
library(DT)

my_theme <- theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    text             = element_text(color = "#2c3e50"),
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5, color = "#7f8c8d"),
    plot.caption     = element_text(size = 9, color = "#95a5a6", hjust = 0),
    axis.title       = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    axis.text        = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1),
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    legend.title     = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "#ecf0f1", linewidth = 0.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "#f8f9fa", color = NA),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

std_opts <- list(
  opts_tooltip(
    css = paste0("background-color:#2c3e50;color:white;padding:8px 12px;",
                 "border-radius:6px;font-size:12px;line-height:1.5;")
  ),
  opts_hover(css = "fill-opacity:0.85;stroke:white;stroke-width:1;"),
  opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;"),
  opts_zoom(min = 0.5, max = 3),
  opts_toolbar(position = "topright"),
  opts_sizing(rescale = TRUE, width = 1)
)

title: India’s Foodgrain Buffer Stocks: Trends, Norms, and Policy
text:
2 Data Loading and Preparation


Code
## Load current stocks data
stocksall <- fread("~/stocks/stocksall.csv", sep = ",", header = TRUE)
stocksall <- unique(stocksall)

## Load historical data
oldstocks <- fread("~/stocks/pdsold.csv", sep = ",", header = TRUE)
oldstocks[, coarsegrains := stocks_total - stocks_rice - stocks_wheat]

## Reshape current stocks to long format
stocks_long <- melt(stocksall, id = c("Year", "Commodity"))
stocks_long[, Date := as.Date(paste("01", variable, Year, sep = "-"), "%d-%b-%Y")]
stocks_long[, Commodity := factor(Commodity,
  levels = c("Rice", "Wheat", "Unmilled Paddy", "Coarsegrain", "Total"))]

## Process historical data
oldstocks_clean <- separate(oldstocks, col = Year, sep = "-", into = c("v1", "year"))
setDT(oldstocks_clean)
oldstocks_clean[, Date := as.Date(paste("01", "04", year, sep = "-"), "%d-%m-%y")]
oldstocks_clean <- oldstocks_clean[Date < as.Date("2003-01-01"),
  .(Date, Rice = stocks_rice, Wheat = stocks_wheat, Coarsegrain = coarsegrains)]
old_long <- melt(oldstocks_clean, id = "Date", variable.name = "Commodity")
old_long[, value := value * 10]   ## → lakh tonnes
old_long[, Commodity := as.character(Commodity)]

## Filter and combine
new_long <- stocks_long[Commodity != "Total", .(Date, Commodity = as.character(Commodity), value)]
new_long <- new_long[!is.na(value)]
old_long <- old_long[!is.na(value)]

combined <- rbind(new_long, old_long)
combined[, value := as.numeric(value) / 10]   ## → million tonnes
combined <- combined[!is.na(value)]
combined[, Commodity := factor(Commodity,
  levels = rev(c("Rice", "Wheat", "Unmilled Paddy", "Coarsegrain")))]

## Build tooltip
combined[, tip := paste0("<b>", Commodity, "</b><br>",
                         "Date: ", format(Date, "%b %Y"), "<br>",
                         "Stock: <b>", round(value, 2), " MT</b>")]

title: India’s Foodgrain Buffer Stocks: Trends, Norms, and Policy
text:
3 Full Historical Stock Position (1972–2025)


Code
p_full <- ggplot(combined, aes(x = Date, y = value, fill = Commodity,
                                tooltip = tip, data_id = paste(Date, Commodity),
                                group = Commodity)) +
  geom_area_interactive(alpha = 0.85, color = "white", linewidth = 0.3,
                        position = "stack") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Rice"          = "#4E79A7",
    "Wheat"         = "#F28E2B",
    "Unmilled Paddy" = "#E15759",
    "Coarsegrain"   = "#76B7B2"
  )) +
  ## Mark major policy events
  annotate("rect", xmin = as.Date("2001-01-01"), xmax = as.Date("2003-01-01"),
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, fill = "#e74c3c", alpha = 0.07) +
  annotate("text", x = as.Date("2002-01-01"), y = 75,
           label = "2002\nDrought", size = 3, color = "#c0392b", angle = 90) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "5 years", date_labels = "%Y") +
  scale_y_continuous("Stocks (Million Tonnes)", labels = comma_format(),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  labs(title   = "India's Central Pool Foodgrain Stock Position (1972–2025)",
       subtitle = "Stacked area — hover for commodity breakdown by date",
       x = "Year",
       caption = "Source: Food Corporation of India (FCI) | Analysis: Pawan") +
  my_theme

girafe(ggobj = p_full, width_svg = 14, height_svg = 7, options = std_opts)

title: India’s Foodgrain Buffer Stocks: Trends, Norms, and Policy
text:
4 Recent Trends: 2018–2025


Code
recent <- combined[Date >= as.Date("2018-01-01")]
recent[, tip_r := paste0("<b>", Commodity, "</b><br>",

title: India’s Foodgrain Buffer Stocks: Trends, Norms, and Policy
text:
6 Last 24 Months — Interactive Data Table


Code
last24 <- combined[Date >= as.Date("2023-04-01")]
last24_wide <- dcast(last24, Date ~ Commodity, value.var = "value", fun.aggregate = sum)
last24_wide[, Total := rowSums(.SD, na.rm = TRUE), .SDcols = setdiff(names(last24_wide), "Date")]
last24_wide[, Date := format(Date, "%b-%Y")]

datatable(
  last24_wide,
  rownames = FALSE,
  extensions = "Buttons",
  options = list(
    pageLength = 12,
    dom = "Bfrtip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    scrollX = TRUE
  ),
  caption = htmltools::tags$caption(
    style = "caption-side:top;text-align:center;font-size:15px;font-weight:bold;",
    "Central Pool Foodgrain Stocks — Last 24 Months (Million Tonnes)"
  )
) |>
  formatRound(columns = setdiff(names(last24_wide), "Date"), digits = 2)

title: India’s Foodgrain Buffer Stocks: Trends, Norms, and Policy
text:
7 Key Takeaways






ImportantSummary of Findings




India’s central pool stocks are at historically high levels relative to buffer norms, reflecting several consecutive good harvests and aggressive procurement — but also significant storage infrastructure pressure and fiscal carrying costs.
Wheat stocks follow a pronounced seasonal cycle: they decline through the year as PDS offtake and open market sales proceed, then jump sharply in April–June as the rabi wheat crop is procured. The April 2024 dip to ~7.5 LMT wheat was the lowest in nearly a decade — a reminder that even apparently comfortable stock positions can dissipate rapidly.
Rice and unmilled paddy stocks have grown substantially, driven by the expansion of paddy procurement from eastern states (Chhattisgarh, Telangana, Odisha, West Bengal) under the Decentralised Procurement scheme.
Unmilled paddy holdings represent a distinctive feature of India’s system: paddy is often stored unmilled until conversion to rice is needed, creating a large but operationally more complex buffer than milled rice holdings.
COVID-19 (2020) did not disrupt stocks — the government used its large pre-pandemic stock position to mount the PM-GKAY free grain distribution scheme, demonstrating the food security value of the buffer stock system.
The coarse grain stock position remains negligible despite policy attention to millets — the procurement system for coarse grains outside a few states remains underdeveloped.

title: India’s Foodgrain Supply Chain: Procurement, Buffer Stocks, and Trade — An Integrated Analysis
text:
India’s foodgrain supply chain is one of the largest and most complex public food economy systems in the world. It links 140 million farming households at one end to 800 million subsidy beneficiaries at the other — through a sequence of MSP procurement, FCI storage, buffer stock management, PDS distribution, and selective open market sales or exports. This analysis brings together three previously separate datasets — procurement records, FCI stock positions, and trade statistics — to trace the complete chain and identify where the system creates value and where it generates costs and inefficiencies.







TipKey Questions Addressed




How has procurement grown relative to buffer stock norms?
What is the relationship between stock build-up and export decisions?
How have India’s cereal export/import patterns tracked against domestic stock cycles?
Which crops show the tightest (and loosest) supply chain efficiency?

title: India’s Foodgrain Supply Chain: Procurement, Buffer Stocks, and Trade — An Integrated Analysis
text:
1 Setup


Code
library(data.table); library(ggplot2); library(ggiraph)
library(scales); library(gt); library(viridis); library(readxl)
library(lubridate); library(tidyr); library(DT)

my_theme <- theme_minimal(base_size=13)+
  theme(text=element_text(color="#2c3e50"),
        plot.title=element_text(size=16,face="bold",hjust=0.5),
        plot.subtitle=element_text(size=11,hjust=0.5,color="#7f8c8d"),
        plot.caption=element_text(size=9,color="#95a5a6",hjust=0),
        axis.title=element_text(size=12,face="bold"),
        axis.text=element_text(size=10),
        axis.text.x=element_text(angle=45,vjust=1,hjust=1),
        legend.position="bottom",legend.title=element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major=element_line(color="#ecf0f1",linewidth=0.4),
        panel.grid.minor=element_blank(),
        strip.background=element_rect(fill="#f8f9fa",color=NA),
        strip.text=element_text(face="bold",size=11))

std_opts <- list(
  opts_tooltip(css=paste0("background-color:#2c3e50;color:white;padding:8px 12px;",
               "border-radius:6px;font-size:12px;line-height:1.5;")),
  opts_hover(css="fill-opacity:0.85;stroke:white;stroke-width:1.5;"),
  opts_hover_inv(css="opacity:0.2;"),
  opts_zoom(min=0.5,max=3), opts_toolbar(position="topright"),
  opts_sizing(rescale=TRUE,width=1))

title: India’s Foodgrain Supply Chain: Procurement, Buffer Stocks, and Trade — An Integrated Analysis
text:
2 Data Loading


Code
## Procurement
proc <- fread("/home/pawan/procurement/procurement.csv")

## Stocks
stocksall <- unique(fread("~/stocks/stocksall.csv"))
oldstocks  <- fread("~/stocks/pdsold.csv")
oldstocks[,coarsegrains:=stocks_total-stocks_rice-stocks_wheat]
stocks_long <- melt(stocksall,id=c("Year","Commodity"))
stocks_long[,Date:=as.Date(paste("01",variable,Year,sep="-"),"%d-%b-%Y")]
stocks_long[,Commodity:=factor(Commodity,
  levels=c("Rice","Wheat","Unmilled Paddy","Coarsegrain","Total"))]

## Trade
qty_exp  <- as.data.table(readRDS("/home/pawan/trade/totaltrade/total_exportsqty.rds"))
qty_imp  <- as.data.table(readRDS("/home/pawan/trade/totaltrade/total_importsqty.rds"))
usd_exp  <- as.data.table(readRDS("/home/pawan/trade/totaltrade/total_exportsusd.rds"))
usd_imp  <- as.data.table(readRDS("/home/pawan/trade/totaltrade/total_importsusd.rds"))
for(dt in list(qty_exp,qty_imp,usd_exp,usd_imp)) {
  dt[,HSCode2:=substr(HSCode,1,2)]
  dt[,HSCode4:=substr(HSCode,1,4)] }
qty_exp[,Export:=as.numeric(gsub(",","",Export))][is.na(Export),Export:=0]
qty_imp[,Import:=as.numeric(gsub(",","",Import))][is.na(Import),Import:=0]
usd_exp[,Export:=as.numeric(gsub(",","",Export))][is.na(Export),Export:=0]
usd_imp[,Import:=as.numeric(gsub(",","",Import))][is.na(Import),Import:=0]

title: India’s Foodgrain Supply Chain: Procurement, Buffer Stocks, and Trade — An Integrated Analysis
text:
3 Part 1: Procurement Trends

3.1 Total Procurement by Crop


Code
proc_sum <- proc[,.(Total=sum(Total,na.rm=TRUE),FCI=sum(FCI,na.rm=TRUE),
                    State=sum(StateAgency,na.rm=TRUE)),by=.(Crop,MarketingSeason)]
proc_sum[,FCI_pct:=round(FCI*100/Total)][,State_pct:=round(State*100/Total)]
proc_sum[,tip:=paste0("<b>",Crop,"</b><br>Season: ",MarketingSeason,
  "<br>Total: <b>",round(Total,1)," LMT</b><br>FCI: ",round(FCI,1),
  " (",FCI_pct,"%)<br>State: ",round(State,1)," (",State_pct,"%)")]

pp <- ggplot(proc_sum,aes(x=MarketingSeason,y=Total,fill=Crop,
                           tooltip=tip,data_id=paste(Crop,MarketingSeason)))+
  geom_bar_interactive(stat="identity",width=0.75,alpha=0.9)+
  facet_wrap(~Crop,scales="free_y")+
  scale_fill_viridis(discrete=TRUE,option="D")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=comma_format())+
  labs(y="Quantity (Lakh Metric Tonnes)",x="Marketing Season",
       caption="Source: DFPD Procurement Portal | Analysis: Pawan")+my_theme+
  theme(legend.position="none")
girafe(ggobj=pp,width_svg=12,height_svg=7,options=std_opts)








Figure 1: India’s Foodgrain Procurement by Crop and Marketing Season (Lakh Metric Tonnes)






3.2 Procurement Summary Table


Code
proc_wide <- dcast(proc_sum[Crop %in% c("Wheat","Paddy")],
                   MarketingSeason~Crop,value.var="Total",fun.aggregate=sum)
proc_wide[,Grand_Total:=rowSums(.SD,na.rm=TRUE),.SDcols=c("Wheat","Paddy")]
proc_wide[,YoY_Wheat:=round((Wheat/shift(Wheat)-1)*100,1)]
proc_wide[,YoY_Paddy:=round((Paddy/shift(Paddy)-1)*100,1)]
gt(proc_wide)|>
  tab_header(title=md("**Wheat & Paddy Procurement (LMT)**"),
             subtitle="FCI + State Agencies; YoY = year-on-year % change")|>
  fmt_number(columns=c(Wheat,Paddy,Grand_Total),decimals=1)|>
  fmt_number(columns=c(YoY_Wheat,YoY_Paddy),decimals=1)|>
  cols_label(MarketingSeason="Season",Grand_Total="Total",
             YoY_Wheat="Wheat YoY%",YoY_Paddy="Paddy YoY%")|>
  data_color(columns=Wheat,palette=c("#eff6ff","#bfdbfe","#1d4ed8"))|>
  data_color(columns=Paddy,palette=c("#f0fdf4","#86efac","#16a34a"))|>

title: India’s Foodgrain Supply Chain: Procurement, Buffer Stocks, and Trade — An Integrated Analysis
text:
4 Part 2: Buffer Stock Position

4.1 Long-Run Stock History


Code
old_clean <- separate(oldstocks,col=Year,sep="-",into=c("v1","year"))
setDT(old_clean)
old_clean[,Date:=as.Date(paste("01","04",year,sep="-"),"%d-%m-%y")]
old_clean <- old_clean[Date<as.Date("2003-01-01"),
  .(Date,Rice=stocks_rice,Wheat=stocks_wheat,Coarsegrain=coarsegrains)]
old_long <- melt(old_clean,id="Date",variable.name="Commodity")
old_long[,value:=value*10][,Commodity:=as.character(Commodity)]
new_long <- stocks_long[Commodity!="Total",.(Date,Commodity=as.character(Commodity),value)]
combined_stocks <- rbind(new_long[!is.na(value)],old_long[!is.na(value)])
combined_stocks[,value:=as.numeric(value)/10]
combined_stocks[,Commodity:=factor(Commodity,
  levels=rev(c("Rice","Wheat","Unmilled Paddy","Coarsegrain")))]
combined_stocks[,tip:=paste0("<b>",Commodity,"</b><br>",format(Date,"%b %Y"),
  "<br>Stock: <b>",round(value,2)," MT</b>")]

ps <- ggplot(combined_stocks,aes(x=Date,y=value,fill=Commodity,
                                  tooltip=tip,data_id=paste(Date,Commodity),group=Commodity))+
  geom_area_interactive(alpha=0.85,color="white",linewidth=0.3,position="stack")+
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("Rice"="#4E79A7","Wheat"="#F28E2B",
                             "Unmilled Paddy"="#E15759","Coarsegrain"="#76B7B2"))+
  scale_x_date(date_breaks="5 years",date_labels="%Y")+
  scale_y_continuous("Stocks (Million Tonnes)",labels=comma_format(),
                     expand=expansion(mult=c(0,0.05)))+
  labs(x="Year",caption="Source: Food Corporation of India | Analysis: Pawan")+my_theme
girafe(ggobj=ps,width_svg=14,height_svg=7,options=std_opts)








Figure 2: India’s Central Pool Foodgrain Stocks 1972–2025 (Million Tonnes) — Stacked Area






4.2 Recent Stock Trends (2018–2025)


Code
recent_stocks <- combined_stocks[Date>=as.Date("2018-01-01")]
pr <- ggplot(recent_stocks,aes(x=Date,y=value,fill=Commodity,
                                tooltip=tip,data_id=paste(Date,Commodity),group=Commodity))+
  geom_area_interactive(alpha=0.85,color="white",linewidth=0.3,position="stack")+
  geom_vline(xintercept=as.Date("2020-03-25"),linetype="dashed",color="#e74c3c",linewidth=0.7)+
  annotate("text",x=as.Date("2020-06-01"),y=55,label="COVID\nLockdown",
           size=3,color="#e74c3c",fontface="bold")+
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("Rice"="#4E79A7","Wheat"="#F28E2B",
                             "Unmilled Paddy"="#E15759","Coarsegrain"="#76B7B2"))+
  scale_x_date(date_breaks="6 months",date_labels="%b %Y")+
  scale_y_continuous("Stocks (Million Tonnes)",labels=comma_format(),
                     expand=expansion(mult=c(0,0.05)))+
  labs(x="Month-Year",caption="Source: FCI | Analysis: Pawan")+
  my_theme+theme(axis.text.x=element_text(size=8))
girafe(ggobj=pr,width_svg=14,height_svg=7,options=std_opts)








Figure 3: Recent Stock Trends 2018–2025 — Seasonal Wheat Cycle and Growing Paddy Holdings

Code

last24_wide <- dcast(last24,Date~Commodity,value.var="value",fun.aggregate=sum)
last24_wide[,Total:=rowSums(.SD,na.rm=TRUE),.SDcols=setdiff(names(last24_wide),"Date")]
last24_wide[,Date:=format(Date,"%b-%Y")]
datatable(last24_wide,rownames=FALSE,
  options=list(pageLength=12,dom="Bfrtip",buttons=c("copy","csv"),scrollX=TRUE),
  caption=htmltools::tags$caption(
    style="caption-side:top;text-align:center;font-size:14px;font-weight:bold;",
    "Central Pool Stocks — Last 24 Months (Million Tonnes)"))|>
  formatRound(columns=setdiff(names(last24_wide),"Date"),digits=2)

title: India’s Foodgrain Supply Chain: Procurement, Buffer Stocks, and Trade — An Integrated Analysis
section: 5 Part 3: Cereal Trade — Export and Import Flows
text:
5 Part 3: Cereal Trade — Export and Import Flows

5.1 Cereal Export Composition


Code
cer_exp <- qty_exp[HSCode2=="10"]
cer_exp[,Commodity:=fcase(HSCode4=="1006","Rice",HSCode4=="1001","Wheat",
  HSCode4=="1005","Maize",HSCode4=="1008","Millet",default="Other")]
cer_yr <- cer_exp[,.(Qty=sum(Export,na.rm=TRUE)),by=.(Year,Commodity)]
cer_yr[,Total:=sum(Qty),by=Year][,Share:=round(Qty*100/Total)]
cer_yr[,Commodity:=factor(Commodity,levels=c("Other","Millet","Maize","Wheat","Rice"))]
cer_yr[,tip:=paste0("<b>",Commodity,"</b><br>Year: ",Year,"<br>",
  "Qty: ",format(round(Qty),big.mark=",")," '000T<br>Share: ",Share,"%")]

pce <- ggplot(cer_yr,aes(x=Year,y=Qty,fill=Commodity,tooltip=tip,data_id=paste(Commodity,Year)))+
  geom_bar_interactive(stat="identity",position="stack",width=0.75,alpha=0.9)+
  geom_text(aes(label=ifelse(Share>=8,paste0(Share,"%"),"")),
            position=position_stack(vjust=0.5),size=2.8,color="white",fontface="bold")+
  scale_fill_viridis(discrete=TRUE,option="D")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=comma_format())+
  labs(y="Quantity ('000 Tonnes)",x="Year",
       caption="Source: Ministry of Commerce | Analysis: Pawan")+my_theme
girafe(ggobj=pce,width_svg=11,height_svg=6,options=std_opts)








Figure 4: India’s Cereal Exports: Rice Dominates, Wheat is Policy-Driven






5.2 Cereal Export-Import Balance


Code
cer_exp_yr <- cer_yr[,.(Exp_Qty=sum(Qty)),by=Year]
cer_imp <- qty_imp[HSCode2=="10",.(Imp_Qty=sum(Import,na.rm=TRUE)),by=Year]
cer_bal <- merge(cer_exp_yr,cer_imp,by="Year",all=TRUE)
cer_bal[is.na(Imp_Qty),Imp_Qty:=0][is.na(Exp_Qty),Exp_Qty:=0]
cer_bal[,Balance:=Exp_Qty-Imp_Qty]
cer_bal[,tip:=paste0("Year: ",Year,"<br>Exports: ",format(round(Exp_Qty),big.mark=","),
  " '000T<br>Imports: ",format(round(Imp_Qty),big.mark=","),
  " '000T<br>Balance: <b>",format(round(Balance),big.mark=","),"</b>")]

long_cb <- melt(cer_bal[,.(Year,Exp_Qty,Imp_Qty)],id="Year",
                variable.name="Flow",value.name="Qty")
long_cb[,ftip:=paste0(ifelse(Flow=="Exp_Qty","<b>Exports</b>","<b>Imports</b>"),
  "<br>Year: ",Year,"<br>",format(round(Qty),big.mark=",")," '000T")]

pb <- ggplot()+
  geom_col_interactive(data=cer_bal,aes(x=Year,y=Balance,tooltip=tip,data_id=Year,
                                         fill=Balance>0),width=0.7,alpha=0.35)+
  geom_line_interactive(data=long_cb,aes(x=Year,y=Qty,color=Flow,tooltip=ftip,
                                          data_id=paste(Flow,Year),group=Flow),linewidth=1.3)+
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("TRUE"="#27ae60","FALSE"="#e74c3c"),guide="none")+
  scale_color_manual(values=c("Exp_Qty"="#27ae60","Imp_Qty"="#e74c3c"),
                     labels=c("Exports","Imports"))+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=comma_format())+
  labs(y="Quantity ('000 Tonnes)",x="Year",
       subtitle="Green bar = net exporter; red bar = net importer; lines show absolute flows",
       caption="Source: Ministry of Commerce | Analysis: Pawan")+my_theme
girafe(ggobj=pb,width_svg=11,height_svg=6,options=std_opts)








Figure 5: India’s Cereal Export vs. Import: Mostly a Net Exporter, Rarely Imports






5.3 Cereal Trade Table


Code
cb_tbl <- cer_bal[,.(Year,Exports=round(Exp_Qty),Imports=round(Imp_Qty),Balance=round(Balance))]
gt(cb_tbl)|>
  tab_header(title=md("**India's Cereal Trade Balance ('000 Tonnes)**"))|>
  fmt_number(columns=c(Exports,Imports,Balance),decimals=0,use_seps=TRUE)|>
  data_color(columns=Balance,palette=c("#fee2e2","#fca5a5","#dc2626","#fff","#86efac","#16a34a"))|>
  tab_source_note("Source: Ministry of Commerce | Analysis: Pawan")

title: India’s Foodgrain Supply Chain: Procurement, Buffer Stocks, and Trade — An Integrated Analysis
section: 6 Part 4: Supply Chain Integration — Linking Procurement, Stocks, and Trade
text:
6 Part 4: Supply Chain Integration — Linking Procurement, Stocks, and Trade

6.1 Procurement and Rice Stocks: Are They Moving Together?


Code
paddy_proc <- proc[Crop=="Paddy",.(Proc_LMT=sum(Total,na.rm=TRUE)),by=MarketingSeason]
paddy_proc[,Year:=as.numeric(substr(MarketingSeason,1,4))]

rice_apr <- stocks_long[Commodity=="Rice"&!is.na(value),
  .(Stock_MT=mean(as.numeric(value),na.rm=TRUE)/10),by=.(Year=year(Date))]

intg <- merge(paddy_proc[,.(Year,Proc_LMT)],rice_apr,by="Year",all.inner=TRUE)
intg[,tip:=paste0("Year: ",Year,"<br>Paddy proc: ",round(Proc_LMT,1),
  " LMT<br>Rice stock (Apr): ",round(Stock_MT,2)," MT")]

pi <- ggplot(intg,aes(x=Proc_LMT,y=Stock_MT,tooltip=tip,data_id=Year))+
  geom_point_interactive(size=5,alpha=0.85,color="#e67e22")+
  geom_smooth(method="lm",se=TRUE,color="#2980b9",linewidth=1.2,fill="#bde0fe",alpha=0.3)+
  geom_text(aes(label=Year),vjust=-0.9,size=3.5,color="#2c3e50")+
  scale_x_continuous(labels=comma_format())+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=label_number(suffix=" MT"))+
  labs(x="Paddy Procurement (LMT)",y="Rice Stocks in April (MT)",
       subtitle="Positive relationship confirms: more procurement → higher stock position",
       caption="Source: DFPD; FCI | Analysis: Pawan")+my_theme
girafe(ggobj=pi,width_svg=10,height_svg=6,options=std_opts)








Figure 6: Paddy Procurement vs. Rice Stock Position — Testing Supply Chain Linkage






6.2 Annual Overview Table: Procurement + Stocks + Exports


Code
wheat_proc <- proc[Crop=="Wheat",.(Proc_LMT=round(sum(Total,na.rm=TRUE),1)),by=MarketingSeason]
wheat_proc[,Year:=as.numeric(substr(MarketingSeason,1,4))]
wheat_stock_apr <- stocks_long[Commodity=="Wheat"&!is.na(value),
  .(Stock_MT=round(mean(as.numeric(value),na.rm=TRUE)/10,2)),by=.(Year=year(Date))]
wheat_exp_yr <- qty_exp[HSCode4=="1001",.(Wheat_Exp=round(sum(Export,na.rm=TRUE)/1000,1)),by=Year]
wheat_exp_yr[,Year:=as.numeric(Year)]

intg2 <- Reduce(function(a,b) merge(a,b,by="Year",all=TRUE),
                list(wheat_proc[,.(Year,Proc_LMT)],wheat_stock_apr,wheat_exp_yr))
gt(intg2[!is.na(Proc_LMT)])|>
  tab_header(title=md("**Wheat Supply Chain: Procurement → Stocks → Exports**"),
             subtitle="Procurement in LMT; Stocks in MT; Exports in MT ('000 tonnes)")|>
  cols_label(Year="Year",Proc_LMT="Procurement (LMT)",
             Stock_MT="April Stocks (MT)",Wheat_Exp="Wheat Exports (MT)")|>
  fmt_number(columns=c(Proc_LMT,Stock_MT,Wheat_Exp),decimals=1)|>
  data_color(columns=Proc_LMT,palette=c("#eff6ff","#bfdbfe","#1d4ed8"))|>
  data_color(columns=Stock_MT,palette=c("#f0fdf4","#86efac","#16a34a"))|>
  tab_source_note("Source: DFPD; FCI; Ministry of Commerce | Analysis: Pawan")

NoteReading the Integrated Table



The table traces the wheat supply chain end-to-end: procurement in the April–June rabi season builds up stocks visible in April-month stock positions, and excess stocks are released through exports (when permitted) or domestic open market sales (OMSS).
