title: India’s Foodgrain Supply Chain: Procurement, Buffer Stocks, and Trade — An Integrated Analysis
text:
7 Key Takeaways






ImportantIntegrated Supply Chain Findings




Procurement has grown faster than buffer norms: wheat procurement crossed 40 LMT and paddy exceeded 600 LMT in recent seasons — well above the buffer stock norms set by the government, creating storage pressure and fiscal carrying costs.
The procurement-stocks-export chain is empirically visible: the scatter plot shows a strong positive relationship between annual paddy procurement and subsequent rice stock positions.
India uses surplus stocks as an export safety valve: in years of strong wheat stocks (2021–22), India permitted large wheat exports; it reversed this with an export ban when domestic prices rose.
Coarse grain procurement remains minimal despite rising domestic millet consumption and the government’s International Year of Millets 2023 push.
The COVID-19 period demonstrated the system’s value: large pre-pandemic stocks enabled PMGKAY free grain distribution to 800 million people for over two years — a genuine food security achievement enabled by the buffer stock system.

title: India’s Import Concentration Risk: A Cross-Commodity Analysis
text:
India’s import dependence is well-documented, but the geopolitical concentration of that dependence — how many countries supply each critical import — is less frequently quantified. A high concentration means that a single country’s export restriction, natural disaster, or political crisis can immediately disrupt India’s supply. This analysis uses the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) — a standard measure of market concentration — to rank India’s import categories by geopolitical risk, and tracks how concentration has evolved over time.
HHI is calculated as the sum of squared shares: HHI = Σ(sᵢ²) where sᵢ is each supplier’s percentage share. Values range from 0 (perfectly diversified) to 10,000 (single supplier). By convention: <1,500 = low concentration; 1,500–2,500 = moderate; >2,500 = high concentration.







TipKey Findings Preview




🔴 Phosphoric acid imports are the most concentrated — Morocco supplies >70%, giving HHI >5,000
🟠 MOP/potash and sunflower oil carry high concentration risk (HHI >3,000)
🟡 Urea has the most improved diversification since 2021 (forced diversification from China)
🟢 Palm oil concentration is surprisingly stable — Indonesia+Malaysia consistently >90% but this is a stable duopoly
📉 Most categories improved diversification in 2022–24 as India scrambled to manage geopolitical supply shocks

title: India’s Import Concentration Risk: A Cross-Commodity Analysis
text:
1 Setup


Code
library(data.table); library(ggplot2); library(ggiraph)
library(scales); library(gt); library(viridis)

my_theme <- theme_minimal(base_size=13)+
  theme(text=element_text(color="#2c3e50"),
        plot.title=element_text(size=16,face="bold",hjust=0.5),
        plot.subtitle=element_text(size=11,hjust=0.5,color="#7f8c8d"),
        plot.caption=element_text(size=9,color="#95a5a6",hjust=0),
        axis.title=element_text(size=12,face="bold"),
        axis.text=element_text(size=10),
        axis.text.x=element_text(angle=45,vjust=1,hjust=1),
        legend.position="bottom",legend.title=element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major=element_line(color="#ecf0f1",linewidth=0.4),
        panel.grid.minor=element_blank(),
        strip.background=element_rect(fill="#f8f9fa",color=NA),
        strip.text=element_text(face="bold"))

std_opts <- list(
  opts_tooltip(css=paste0("background-color:#2c3e50;color:white;padding:8px 12px;",
               "border-radius:6px;font-size:12px;line-height:1.5;")),
  opts_hover(css="fill-opacity:0.85;stroke:white;stroke-width:1.5;"),
  opts_hover_inv(css="opacity:0.2;"),
  opts_zoom(min=0.5,max=3), opts_toolbar(position="topright"),
  opts_sizing(rescale=TRUE,width=1))

## HHI function
hhi <- function(shares) round(sum((shares/sum(shares)*100)^2))

title: India’s Import Concentration Risk: A Cross-Commodity Analysis
text:
2 Data Loading and HHI Computation

2.1 Fertilizer Concentration


Code
t1 <- readRDS("/home/pawan/fertilizer/urea_importqty.rds")
t2 <- readRDS("/home/pawan/fertilizer/dap_importqty.rds")
t3 <- readRDS("/home/pawan/fertilizer/rock_phosphate_importqty.rds")
t4 <- readRDS("/home/pawan/fertilizer/phosphoric_acid_importqty.rds")
t5 <- readRDS("/home/pawan/fertilizer/potash_importqty.rds")
for(d in list(t1,t2,t3,t4,t5)) d[,ImportMonth:=gsub(",","",ImportMonth)]

calc_hhi_fert <- function(dt, label) {
  out <- dt[Country!="Total",.(Qty=sum(as.numeric(ImportMonth),na.rm=TRUE)),by=.(Country,Year)]
  out <- out[Year>"2015"]
  out[,HHI:=hhi(Qty),by=Year]
  out[,.(HHI=first(HHI)),by=Year][,Category:=label] }

hhi_urea  <- calc_hhi_fert(t1,"Urea")
hhi_dap   <- calc_hhi_fert(t2,"DAP")
hhi_rock  <- calc_hhi_fert(t3,"Rock Phosphate")
hhi_phos  <- calc_hhi_fert(t4,"Phosphoric Acid")
hhi_mop   <- calc_hhi_fert(t5,"MOP (Potash)")
hhi_fert  <- rbindlist(list(hhi_urea,hhi_dap,hhi_rock,hhi_phos,hhi_mop))




2.2 Edible Oil Concentration


Code
oil_year_fn <- function(dt) {
  dt[,Month_num:=match(Month,month.abb)]
  dt[,Fin_Year:=ifelse(Month_num>10,paste0(Year,"-",substr(as.numeric(Year)+1,3,4)),
                       paste0(as.numeric(Year)-1,"-",substr(Year,3,4)))]
  dt }

palmoil_qty   <- oil_year_fn(readRDS("/home/pawan/edibleoils/palmoil_importqty.rds"))
soybeanoil_qty <- oil_year_fn(readRDS("/home/pawan/edibleoils/soybeanoil_importqty.rds"))
sunfloweroil_qty <- oil_year_fn(readRDS("/home/pawan/edibleoils/sunfloweroil_importqty.rds"))
for(d in list(palmoil_qty,soybeanoil_qty,sunfloweroil_qty)) d[,ImportMonth:=gsub(",","",ImportMonth)]

calc_hhi_oil <- function(dt, label) {
  out <- dt[Country!="Total",.(Qty=sum(as.numeric(ImportMonth),na.rm=TRUE)),by=.(Country,Fin_Year)]
  out <- out[Fin_Year>="2010-11"&Fin_Year!="2024-25"]
  out[,HHI:=hhi(Qty),by=Fin_Year]
  out[,.(HHI=first(HHI)),by=Fin_Year][,Category:=label][,Year:=substr(Fin_Year,1,4)] }

hhi_palm <- calc_hhi_oil(palmoil_qty,"Palm Oil")
hhi_soy  <- calc_hhi_oil(soybeanoil_qty,"Soybean Oil")
hhi_sf   <- calc_hhi_oil(sunfloweroil_qty,"Sunflower Oil")
hhi_oils <- rbindlist(list(hhi_palm[,.(Year,HHI,Category)],
                            hhi_soy[,.(Year,HHI,Category)],
                            hhi_sf[,.(Year,HHI,Category)]))

title: India’s Import Concentration Risk: A Cross-Commodity Analysis
text:
7 Policy Implications






ImportantConcentration Risk: Priority Actions by Category



Immediate priorities (HHI >3,000):

Phosphoric Acid: Diversify to Tunisia, Senegal; invest in long-term OCP/Morocco supply agreement with price stabilisation clauses.
MOP: Deepen Canada engagement; explore Laos and Turkmenistan as emerging potash sources; build 3-month strategic reserve.
Sunflower Oil: Develop Turkey, Argentina as alternative suppliers; consider establishing Black Sea alternative routing through Romania/Bulgaria.

Medium-term priorities (HHI 1,500–3,000):

Urea: Maintain post-2021 diversification; preserve OMIFCO relationship; develop Egypt and Iran supply channels contingent on sanctions landscape.
DAP: Expand Morocco OCP relationship; consider joint venture in Moroccan DAP manufacturing.

Structural actions:

Mandate minimum 6-month strategic reserves for all Category A critical imports (fertilizers, edible oils).
Develop a geopolitical supply risk index that is updated quarterly and formally incorporated into import policy decisions.

title: India’s Import Concentration Risk: A Cross-Commodity Analysis
text:
8 Key Takeaways






ImportantSummary of Concentration Analysis




Phosphoric acid and MOP (potash) are India’s highest-risk import categories by HHI measure — both have effective single/duopoly suppliers and both faced severe disruptions in 2021–22.
Sunflower oil carries the fastest-rising concentration risk: its share in the edible oil basket tripled over 14 years, and its supply is now over 80% dependent on the Ukraine-Russia corridor.
Urea concentration has actually improved since 2021 — a case where geopolitical shock (China’s export restriction) forced beneficial diversification.
Palm oil presents a stable duopoly — high concentration (Indonesia+Malaysia ~90%) but from two broadly stable partners with deep commercial relationships with India; the 2022 Indonesia export ban was short-lived.
The 2021–22 commodity crisis was a wake-up call: multiple categories simultaneously experienced supply stress, revealing how India’s concentrated import structure translates into correlated fiscal and food security risks.
A formal strategic reserves and supply diversification policy — modelled on practices in Japan, China, and the EU — is urgently needed for Category A critical imports.

title: The Edible Oil Trap: India’s Deepening Import Dependency and the Road to Self-Sufficiency
text:
Walk into any kitchen in India — from a modest rural home in Rajasthan to a high-rise apartment in Mumbai — and you will find at least one or two bottles of edible oil. Cooking oil is indispensable to Indian cuisine. It is the medium through which spices bloom, vegetables are sautéed, and puris are fried. Yet for all its culinary centrality, India produces far less edible oil than it consumes. Every year, the country imports between 14 and 16 million tonnes of vegetable oils — spending over USD 15–20 billion in foreign exchange — to bridge this gap. The story of India’s edible oil import dependence is not merely an agricultural policy failure; it is a story of geopolitical vulnerability, household inflation, and a decades-long policy reckoning that has yet to be fully resolved.
This article traces the evolution of India’s edible oil import basket from the oil year 2010–11 to 2023–24, examines the structural factors driving import dependence, explores the concentration risks embedded in India’s current supply chains, and assesses the policy levers available to reduce this dependence over the coming decade.

title: Food Price Inflation in India: Retail vs. Wholesale Dynamics and the Household Impact
text:
Food price inflation is never just a macroeconomic statistic in India. For the 800 million people covered under the National Food Security Act and the hundreds of millions more who live just above the subsidy threshold, a 20% increase in the price of dal or a 30% spike in the cost of cooking oil is not an abstraction — it is a direct reduction in nutritional intake, a delayed school fee, or a missed medical appointment. India’s food price system — its architecture of wholesale markets, regulated mandis, state-controlled procurement, and retail supply chains — is one of the most complex in the world, and understanding how prices at the farm gate translate to prices at the retail counter is essential to crafting effective policy responses.
This article analyses India’s food price dynamics across four key commodity groups: cereals, pulses, edible oils, and vegetables. Drawing on retail and wholesale price data compiled from the government’s price monitoring cell, it examines the structure of retail-wholesale price spreads, the seasonal and structural drivers of price volatility, and the differential impact of food inflation on urban and rural households.

title: Food Price Inflation in India: Retail vs. Wholesale Dynamics and the Household Impact
section: The Anatomy of India’s Food Price System
text:
The Anatomy of India’s Food Price System
India’s food pricing operates on two interlinked tracks. The wholesale track reflects prices in regulated agricultural produce market committee (APMC) mandis and wholesale distribution hubs — the price at which traders buy from farmers or import suppliers. The retail track reflects what consumers actually pay in local markets, kirana stores, and modern retail outlets. The gap between these two — the retail-wholesale spread — represents the cumulative cost of storage, transportation, processing, taxation, and the margins of the multiple intermediaries who sit between the farmer and the consumer.
In well-functioning markets, this spread should be stable and relatively narrow, reflecting genuine costs of distribution. In India, however, price spread data reveals a more troubled picture: spreads are often wide, highly variable across states and seasons, and frequently resistant to downward adjustment even when wholesale prices fall. This asymmetric price transmission — wholesale price increases rapidly pass through to retail prices, but wholesale price decreases do not — is a recurrent feature of Indian food markets that imposes a structural cost on consumers.
The government’s price monitoring system collects daily retail prices for 22 essential commodities across 200+ reporting centres, and wholesale prices from major mandis across the country. This granular data reveals patterns of price inflation that aggregate indices like the Consumer Price Index (CPI) — Food often obscure.

title: Food Price Inflation in India: Retail vs. Wholesale Dynamics and the Household Impact
text:
Cereals: The Managed Core
Among the four commodity groups examined here, cereals — primarily rice and wheat — represent the most heavily managed segment of India’s food system. The government sets Minimum Support Prices (MSP) for wheat and paddy annually, procures tens of millions of tonnes through FCI and state agencies, and distributes subsidised cereals to approximately 800 million beneficiaries under the National Food Security Act (NFSA) and subsequently the Pradhan Mantri Garib Kalyan Anna Yojana (PMGKAY).
This system largely insulates the retail price of wheat and rice from international price shocks. When global wheat prices surged in 2022 following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, India’s domestic wheat retail prices rose modestly — in part because the government released stocks from its buffer reserves and banned wheat exports. The rice retail price trajectory has been similarly dampened by government intervention.
However, this managed stability comes at a cost. First, it requires enormous public expenditure — the PMGKAY alone costs the government approximately ₹3.5–4 lakh crore annually in food subsidies. Second, the suppression of market prices discourages private sector investment in cereal post-harvest infrastructure, cold storage, and processing. Third, the gap between PDS issue prices and market prices creates significant leakage, diversion, and “ghost beneficiary” problems that reduce the scheme’s actual welfare impact.
The retail-wholesale spread for rice in India is typically ₹5–8 per kg — roughly 15–20% of the retail price. For wheat, the spread tends to be narrower, at ₹3–5 per kg. These spreads are highest in remote states with poor road connectivity and weakest in states with well-developed APMC infrastructure and private trade networks.

title: Food Price Inflation in India: Retail vs. Wholesale Dynamics and the Household Impact
text:
Pulses: The Volatile Protein Source
If cereals represent the managed, stable core of India’s food price system, pulses represent its volatile, poorly managed periphery. Dal — the protein staple of hundreds of millions of Indian vegetarian households — has experienced some of the most severe price spikes in India’s recent food history. Arhar (tur) dal crossed ₹200 per kg in Delhi in 2023–24, a level that would have been unimaginable a decade earlier. Moong, urad, and masoor have each touched historic highs in various recent years.
The structural reasons for pulse price volatility are well-established. First, India’s domestic pulse production is highly weather-sensitive — pulses are grown predominantly in rainfed areas on residual moisture, making them particularly vulnerable to erratic monsoons. Second, pulse acreage is in constant competition with higher-value crops, and the absence of robust government procurement (until relatively recently) gave farmers weak incentives to maintain pulse cultivation areas. Third, the import regime for pulses has been erratic — the government alternates between imposing import duties (to protect domestic farmers) and suspending them (to control consumer prices), creating uncertainty for both producers and traders.
The retail-wholesale spread for pulses is notably large — typically ₹15–25 per kg, or 20–30% of retail prices. Unlike cereals, where a largely standardised product moves through established supply chains, pulses involve significant post-harvest processing (cleaning, milling, packaging), greater spatial price variation, and more fragmented retail distribution. The dal mill sector — which is largely unorganised and under-capitalised — is a significant contributor to the wide spread.
The government’s response to pulse price spikes has typically been a combination of import duty waivers, release of buffer stock, and export bans. While these measures can dampen acute price spikes, they do not address the structural supply constraints. A more durable solution would involve sustained investment in pulse research (drought-tolerant varieties, better yields), dedicated procurement and price support for pulse farmers, and investment in the dal milling and cold chain infrastructure.

title: Food Price Inflation in India: Retail vs. Wholesale Dynamics and the Household Impact
text:
Edible Oils: Imported Inflation
No commodity group better illustrates the transmission of global price shocks to Indian household budgets than edible oils. As detailed elsewhere in this analysis, India imports 14–16 million tonnes of edible oils annually — accounting for over 50% of its consumption requirement. This structural import dependence means that global edible oil price movements — driven by palm oil supply in Southeast Asia, soybean harvests in South America, or sunflower oil disruptions in the Black Sea region — translate almost directly into Indian retail price changes.
The transmission mechanism operates rapidly and with high fidelity in the upward direction. When international palm oil prices rose sharply in 2021–22, Indian retail palm olein prices in Delhi moved from approximately ₹90/litre to ₹165/litre within six months. The retail-wholesale spread for edible oils is typically ₹5–10 per litre — smaller in proportional terms than pulses, but significant in absolute terms given the large volumes consumed.
Downward price transmission, however, tends to be slower and incomplete. When international edible oil prices fell sharply in the second half of 2022 — palm oil fell from over USD 1,600/MT to below USD 900/MT within months — Indian retail prices declined more gradually, with retailers and distributors adjusting inventory positions before passing the reduction to consumers. This asymmetry systematically favours the supply chain intermediaries at the expense of households.
The government’s key policy tools for edible oil price management are import duty adjustments (duties on crude edible oils have been reduced to near-zero to keep domestic prices in check) and occasional direct market interventions through NAFED. But given the structural nature of India’s import dependence, these tools can only smooth price fluctuations at the margin — they cannot insulate Indian consumers from sustained global price increases.

title: Food Price Inflation in India: Retail vs. Wholesale Dynamics and the Household Impact
text:
Vegetables: The Seasonal Volatility Problem
Vegetables represent perhaps the most spectacularly volatile segment of India’s retail food price landscape. Tomato prices in Delhi famously crossed ₹200 per kg in July–August 2023 — a price so extraordinary that it entered political discourse and became the subject of WhatsApp memes across the country. This was not an isolated event; onion prices have triggered political crises in India on multiple occasions since Independence, and the dramatic seasonal swings in the prices of tomato, potato, and leafy vegetables are an annually recurring phenomenon.
The structure of vegetable price volatility is fundamentally different from that of cereals or pulses. Vegetables are highly perishable, cannot be stored for more than a few days without refrigeration, and are grown in concentrated clusters — Nashik for onions, Pune and Karnataka for tomatoes, Agra for potatoes — that create geographic price transmission problems when these clusters experience adverse weather or pest attacks.
India’s cold chain infrastructure for vegetables remains woefully inadequate relative to production volumes. Approximately 30–40% of the vegetable harvest is estimated to be lost post-harvest due to inadequate storage, poor roads, and lack of refrigerated transport. This wastage drives up the effective cost of the remaining produce, contributing to high retail prices even in years of good harvest. It also means that production surpluses — which should logically reduce prices — often go to waste rather than being distributed to deficit regions.
The retail-wholesale spread for vegetables varies enormously by commodity and season but typically ranges from ₹5–20 per kg — sometimes representing 50–70% of the retail price in markets with fragmented retail and poor road connectivity.

title: Food Price Inflation in India: Retail vs. Wholesale Dynamics and the Household Impact
section: Policy Conclusions: What Works and What Doesn’t
text:
Policy Conclusions: What Works and What Doesn’t
Two decades of food price management experience in India has established a reasonably clear hierarchy of intervention effectiveness.
What works: Strategic public procurement and distribution for storable commodities like wheat, rice, and pulses can effectively dampen price spikes for these specific items. Direct benefit transfers (DBT) to farmers — such as PM-KISAN — provide income support without distorting markets. Investment in cold chain infrastructure, rural roads, and post-harvest management reduces food waste and improves spatial price integration.
What doesn’t work well: Short-term export bans, while effective in damping domestic prices immediately, damage India’s reputation as a reliable trading partner and depress farm-gate prices, creating a disincentive for future production. Import duty waivers are blunt instruments that do not distinguish between emergency needs and normal-year import requirements. Price controls in vegetable markets, when attempted, typically cause supply withdrawal and worsen shortages.
The most durable solution to India’s food inflation problem is structural: higher agricultural productivity, reduced post-harvest losses, better market integration across states, and a more effective safety net for low-income households. These are long-term investments whose payoffs are diffuse and delayed — which is precisely why they tend to be under-prioritised relative to headline-grabbing but ultimately ineffective short-term interventions.

title: The Edible Oil Trap: India’s Deepening Import Dependency and the Road to Self-Sufficiency
text:
Conclusion
India’s edible oil import dependency is the product of decades of policy choices that prioritised cereal self-sufficiency over oilseed production — a choice that made sense in the food-insecure India of the 1960s and 1970s but has left the country structurally exposed to global commodity markets and geopolitical shocks in the twenty-first century. The 2021–22 oil price crisis was a warning. The window to act — through aggressive oilseed production enhancement, oil palm expansion, and strategic reserve creation — is open, but it will not remain so indefinitely. India’s kitchen economy, feeding 1.4 billion people three meals a day, deserves a more resilient foundation.

Data sources: Ministry of Commerce, India (import statistics); Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare (oilseed production and availability data); Solvent Extractors’ Association of India. Analysis by Pawan.

title: India’s Labour Market in Transition: Insights from the Periodic Labour Force Survey
text:
India’s labour market is enormous in scale and complex in structure. With a working-age population (15 years and above) exceeding 900 million people and a labour force of approximately 550–600 million, India is home to one of the world’s largest concentrations of workers. Yet for decades, the quality and frequency of labour market data available to policymakers and researchers was poor. The quinquennial Employment and Unemployment Surveys (EUS) conducted by the National Sample Survey Organisation provided infrequent, though comprehensive, labour market snapshots. The launch of the Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS) in 2017–18 — providing both annual headline estimates and quarterly urban estimates — represented a major methodological upgrade in India’s statistical system.
This article draws on PLFS microdata from four annual rounds (2018–19, 2019–20, 2020–21, and 2021–22) to examine the key dimensions of India’s labour market: labour force participation rates (LFPR), worker force participation rates (WFPR), unemployment rates (UR), and the sectoral composition of employment. The analysis uses the Usual Principal and Subsidiary Status (UPSS) framework — which counts an individual as a worker if they worked for the majority of the reference year as their principal or subsidiary activity — and presents estimates disaggregated by sex (male and female) and sector (rural and urban).

title: India’s Labour Market in Transition: Insights from the Periodic Labour Force Survey
text:
India’s labour market is enormous in scale and complex in structure. With a working-age population (15 years and above) exceeding 900 million people and a labour force of approximately 550–600 million, India is home to one of the world’s largest concentrations of workers. Yet for decades, the quality and frequency of labour market data available to policymakers and researchers was poor. The quinquennial Employment and Unemployment Surveys (EUS) conducted by the National Sample Survey Organisation provided infrequent, though comprehensive, labour market snapshots. The launch of the Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS) in 2017–18 — providing both annual headline estimates and quarterly urban estimates — represented a major methodological upgrade in India’s statistical system.
This article draws on PLFS microdata from four annual rounds (2018–19, 2019–20, 2020–21, and 2021–22) to examine the key dimensions of India’s labour market: labour force participation rates (LFPR), worker force participation rates (WFPR), unemployment rates (UR), and the sectoral composition of employment. The analysis uses the Usual Principal and Subsidiary Status (UPSS) framework — which counts an individual as a worker if they worked for the majority of the reference year as their principal or subsidiary activity — and presents estimates disaggregated by sex (male and female) and sector (rural and urban).

title: India’s Labour Market in Transition: Insights from the Periodic Labour Force Survey
text:
Understanding the PLFS Methodology
Before examining the results, it is important to clarify the methodological framework. The PLFS collects data on each individual’s activity during a reference year, classifying them into one of five broad activity statuses: (11–12) self-employed (own account workers and employers), (21) helper in household enterprise (unpaid family workers), (31) regular salaried/wage employees, (41–51) casual wage workers in public works and other work. Those who are seeking or available for work but not working are classified as unemployed (status code 81).
The Labour Force Participation Rate (LFPR) is defined as the proportion of the working-age population (15+) that is either working or actively seeking employment. The Worker Force Participation Rate (WFPR) counts only those actually employed, excluding job-seekers. The Unemployment Rate (UR) is the proportion of the labour force that is unemployed — i.e., seeking but not finding work.
The UPSS framework is somewhat generous in its classification of workers: a person who worked even one day in a subsidiary capacity during the reference year may be counted as a worker. This means UPSS-based labour market indicators tend to show higher WFPR and lower unemployment rates than the more stringent Current Weekly Status (CWS) or Current Daily Status (CDS) measures. The analysis here uses the UPSS framework.

title: India’s Labour Market in Transition: Insights from the Periodic Labour Force Survey
section: Labour Force Participation: A Low Base, Especially for Women
text:
Labour Force Participation: A Low Base, Especially for Women
India’s LFPR, measured on the UPSS basis, stood at approximately 49–51% across the four survey years (2018–19 to 2021–22). This figure — meaning roughly half of India’s working-age population is either working or looking for work — is lower than the global average and significantly below the rates observed in East Asian economies. Understanding why such a large proportion of India’s working-age population is outside the labour force is one of the central challenges for India’s economic policymakers.
The gender dimension of this picture is stark. India’s female LFPR — one of the lowest in the world among large developing economies — has historically been in the 20–25% range (UPSS basis). The PLFS data from 2018–19 showed female LFPR at approximately 24–26%, with a significant rural-urban gap (rural female LFPR is typically 10–15 percentage points higher than urban female LFPR, because rural women are more likely to be classified as workers through agricultural and household enterprise activities). Male LFPR stands considerably higher at approximately 75–77%, though this too is below what might be expected for an economy at India’s development stage.
The low female LFPR in India reflects a complex interplay of factors: social norms that restrict women’s mobility and constrain their participation in market work; the large share of women’s time absorbed in unpaid domestic and care work (which the PLFS counts as non-work); the relatively low education and skill levels of older cohorts of women; and the lack of safe, appropriately compensated employment opportunities that would attract women into the formal labour market. The paradox is that as education levels among young Indian women have risen dramatically over the past two decades, their measured labour force participation has not risen commensurately — the “education premium” is not yet translating into employment participation in India the way it does in most other economies.

title: India’s Labour Market in Transition: Insights from the Periodic Labour Force Survey
section: The COVID-19 Impact: 2020–21 as a Stress Test
text:
The COVID-19 Impact: 2020–21 as a Stress Test
The PLFS 2020–21 round was conducted during the year of India’s most severe COVID-19 disruption, including the nationwide lockdown of March–May 2020 and the subsequent phased reopening. This round provides a unique window into the labour market impact of the pandemic.
The headline indicators from 2020–21 reveal a complex picture. The aggregate LFPR and WFPR showed significant movements, but not uniformly in the direction one might expect from the severity of the economic disruption. In fact, female LFPR in rural areas increased during 2020–21 — a pattern observed in several COVID-affected economies, explained by the “added worker effect”: when primary earners (typically male wage workers) lose employment, women enter the labour force in distress-driven work, often in agriculture or casual labour.
Urban male WFPR, by contrast, fell — reflecting the severe disruption to urban services, construction, and manufacturing employment during the lockdown period. The formal regular wage employment category — which is most characteristic of urban workers — contracted sharply, while self-employment and casual wage work in rural areas expanded, as migrant workers returned to their home villages and sought agricultural and MGNREGS work.
This “back to agriculture” effect during COVID is consistent with the longstanding role of India’s agricultural sector as an employment absorber of last resort. When the non-farm economy contracts, displaced workers flow back into farming households — not because agricultural productivity or incomes improve, but because the implicit flexibility of family farm labour provides a safety net that India’s formal social protection system cannot fully substitute.

title: India’s Labour Market in Transition: Insights from the Periodic Labour Force Survey
section: Unemployment: Official Rates and the Hidden Problem
text:
Unemployment: Official Rates and the Hidden Problem
India’s officially measured unemployment rate on the UPSS basis has historically been reported as 2–6% — a figure that frequently puzzles observers who see evidence of widespread joblessness, educated youth struggling to find work, and under-employment in the informal sector. The key to understanding this apparent paradox lies in the design of the UPSS methodology itself.
Under the UPSS framework, an individual who works even occasionally in a subsidiary capacity is counted as “employed.” The vast majority of India’s labour force is self-employed — in family farms, petty trade, household enterprises — and virtually none of them are “unemployed” in the UPSS sense, because they can always perform some activity related to the family enterprise. The UPSS-measured unemployment rate, therefore, is best interpreted as capturing open unemployment among educated youth in urban areas — those who are actively seeking formal wage employment and refusing to accept casual or self-employment in the meantime.
This narrow unemployment concept misses the much larger problem of under-employment and disguised unemployment — the situation where workers are nominally employed but at wages and productivity levels far below their potential. The casual agricultural worker who works 120 days per year in the fields is “employed” in UPSS terms; the reality of their labour market situation — low income, high work insecurity, seasonal idleness — is invisible in the headline statistic.
The PLFS data for 2018–19 through 2021–22 shows unemployment rates (UPSS) in the range of 2–4%, with higher rates for educated youth (those who have completed secondary or higher education) and for urban workers. Female unemployment rates are lower than male rates in aggregate terms, partly because women are more likely to accept whatever work is available rather than holding out for formal employment.

title: India’s Labour Market in Transition: Insights from the Periodic Labour Force Survey
section: Sectoral Composition: The Services Revolution and Agricultural Persistence
text:
Sectoral Composition: The Services Revolution and Agricultural Persistence
The sectoral distribution of India’s workforce reveals the persistent importance of agriculture as a source of employment alongside the rapid growth of the services sector. Agriculture — including crop cultivation, animal husbandry, fishing, and forestry — typically employs 45–50% of India’s total workforce on the UPSS basis, even though it contributes only 15–18% of GDP. This gap between agricultural employment share and agricultural GDP share is the classic definition of structural under-employment — too many workers chasing too little value addition.
The manufacturing sector’s share of employment has remained stubbornly low at approximately 11–13% of the workforce — far below what one would expect at India’s income level. India has not achieved the kind of labour-intensive manufacturing expansion that drove employment transformation in China, South Korea, Taiwan, and now Bangladesh and Vietnam. The “missing middle” of India’s industrial structure — the absence of a large, export-oriented labour-intensive manufacturing sector — is one of the most serious structural constraints on India’s ability to absorb its 10–12 million annual labour market entrants into productive employment.
The construction sector employs approximately 12–14% of the workforce — making it India’s second-largest source of non-farm employment. Construction employment is highly sensitive to infrastructure investment cycles and exhibits significant seasonal variation. The COVID lockdown devastated construction employment, and the displacement of construction workers was central to the humanitarian crisis of reverse migration in 2020.
Services — a broad category encompassing everything from domestic work and petty retail to IT services and banking — employ approximately 28–32% of the workforce. Within this category, the distribution is deeply bimodal: a small upper segment of high-skill, high-wage services (IT, finance, professional services) and a very large lower segment of informal, low-wage services (street vending, domestic work, transportation). The celebrated growth of India’s IT and business process services sector is real and important, but it employs only 4–5 million workers — a tiny fraction of the total labour force.

title: India’s Labour Market in Transition: Insights from the Periodic Labour Force Survey
text:
The Rural-Urban Employment Gap
One of the most important dimensions of India’s labour market is the rural-urban divide. Rural workers are more likely to be self-employed in agriculture, more likely to be casual wage workers, less likely to have formal employment contracts, and more likely to be paid in kind (in grain) rather than cash. Urban workers, while also substantially informal, have somewhat higher access to regular wage employment, better wage levels, and greater connectivity to the formal economy.
The PLFS data shows that the urban regular wage employment rate — perhaps the best single indicator of “good jobs” in India — is significantly higher in urban areas (35–40% of urban workers) than in rural areas (10–12% of rural workers). This gap reflects the concentration of large enterprises, public sector employment, and services industries in urban areas.
The rural-urban wage differential is substantial: urban workers earn on average 2–3 times the daily wage of rural workers, after controlling for occupation. This wage gap drives the massive rural-to-urban migration that has characterised India’s urbanisation process — but the pace of urban job creation in formal sectors has not kept up with migration flows, contributing to the expansion of urban informal employment and slum growth.

title: India’s Labour Market in Transition: Insights from the Periodic Labour Force Survey
section: Policy Implications: What the Data Demands
text:
Policy Implications: What the Data Demands
The PLFS data, taken together across the four survey years, points to several priority areas for labour market policy.
Women’s employment is the largest underutilised resource: Increasing female LFPR by even 10 percentage points would add approximately 60–70 million workers to the productive economy — a demographic dividend that India has not yet captured. This requires investments in women’s safety in public spaces and workplaces, expansion of affordable childcare, and the design of flexible part-time work arrangements that accommodate the reality of women’s domestic responsibilities.
The quality of employment matters as much as quantity: India needs not just more jobs but better jobs — regular wage employment with social security coverage, predictable incomes, and career progression potential. The formalisation agenda — extending EPFO coverage, implementing the labour codes, and providing incentives for firms to hire on formal contracts — is central to this objective.
Skill development must be demand-driven: India’s skill development programmes have historically produced certificates without producing employable workers. The disconnect between training content and employer demand — in construction, manufacturing, healthcare, and digital services — must be closed through closer industry-government partnerships and outcome-linked funding for training providers.

title: India’s Labour Market in Transition: Insights from the Periodic Labour Force Survey
text:
Conclusion
The PLFS has been a transformative addition to India’s statistical infrastructure, providing annual insights into a labour market that is simultaneously enormous, diverse, and dynamic. The data from 2018–19 through 2021–22 tells a story of structural challenges — low female participation, agricultural over-reliance, limited formal employment — alongside resilience, as the labour market absorbed the COVID shock better than many feared. The agenda for improving India’s labour market outcomes is clear: more and better jobs, greater female participation, formalisation, and skill development. Executing this agenda at the scale India’s demographics demand is the defining economic challenge of the coming decade.

Data sources: Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS) microdata, Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI), Government of India. Analysis by Pawan.

title: India’s Fertilizer Dependency: Geopolitical Risks, Price Shocks, and the Subsidy Burden
text:
India’s agricultural miracle — the Green Revolution and its subsequent harvests — was built on three pillars: high-yielding variety seeds, irrigation, and chemical fertilizers. Of these three, fertilizers remain the most geopolitically exposed and fiscally costly. India is the world’s second-largest consumer of chemical fertilizers, using approximately 65–70 million nutrient tonnes (MT) of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium annually to sustain the productivity of its 140 million hectares of cultivated land. Yet despite decades of investment in domestic manufacturing, India imports a significant share of its fertilizer requirement — and for certain nutrients, it is almost entirely dependent on foreign sources.
The consequences of this dependence became painfully visible in 2021–22. A perfect storm of post-pandemic demand recovery, Chinese export restrictions on urea and phosphate fertilizers, and the Russia–Ukraine war drove fertilizer prices to multi-decade highs. India’s fertilizer subsidy bill — which the government pays to protect farmers from world price fluctuations — crossed ₹2.5 lakh crore (approximately USD 30 billion) in a single year. This was not merely a budget line item; it was a fiscal emergency that forced a fundamental reassessment of India’s fertilizer policy architecture.
This article traces India’s fertilizer import trajectory, examines the geopolitical concentration risks inherent in its current supply chains, analyses the price shock mechanism and subsidy transmission, and identifies structural reforms that can reduce India’s vulnerability in the years ahead.

title: India’s Fertilizer Dependency: Geopolitical Risks, Price Shocks, and the Subsidy Burden
text:
India’s agricultural miracle — the Green Revolution and its subsequent harvests — was built on three pillars: high-yielding variety seeds, irrigation, and chemical fertilizers. Of these three, fertilizers remain the most geopolitically exposed and fiscally costly. title: India’s Fertilizer Dependency: Geopolitical Risks, Price Shocks, and the Subsidy Burden
section: The Fertilizer Import Portfolio: NPK and Its Dependencies
text:
The Fertilizer Import Portfolio: NPK and Its Dependencies
Chemical fertilizers supply three primary macronutrients — nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K) — each of which India sources through a distinct combination of domestic production and imports.

Nitrogen (Urea): Large but Manageable Domestic Capacity
Urea is the most widely used nitrogenous fertilizer in India, accounting for approximately 50% of total fertilizer consumption by volume. India has a substantial domestic urea manufacturing base — approximately 28–30 MT annual capacity from 30+ plants. In recent years, the government has revived several idle gas-based plants (including at Gorakhpur, Ramagundam, and Sindri) under the New Investment Policy, adding several million tonnes of new capacity. As a result, India now produces approximately 25–27 MT of urea domestically and imports the remaining 6–9 MT.
The import sourcing pattern for urea has been geopolitically interesting. China was historically the dominant supplier, typically providing 30–50% of India’s urea imports. However, China imposed sharp export restrictions in late 2021 to protect domestic supplies, cutting off a major supply source at the worst possible moment — just as global prices were spiking. This forced India to rapidly diversify toward Oman (through OMIFCO, a joint venture in which Indian companies hold significant stakes), Iran, Egypt, and Russia.
This diversification, while forced, has arguably left India in a stronger structural position than before — more geographically balanced sourcing reduces single-country dependency risk. However, three of India’s four major urea suppliers (Iran, Russia, Egypt) face varying degrees of sanctions, political instability, or energy-cost volatility, meaning the diversification is less robust than it superficially appears.


Phosphorus (DAP, Rock Phosphate, Phosphoric Acid): The North African Dependency
Phosphorus-based fertilizers are India’s most concentrated import risk. India has almost no domestic phosphate rock reserves of economic significance — unlike nitrogen, which can be synthesised from atmospheric air using natural gas, or potassium, which at least has some domestic sources in Rajasthan (though of limited economic viability). India’s phosphate supply chain is therefore built entirely on imports.
The hierarchy runs as follows: India imports rock phosphate (primarily from Jordan and Morocco) as raw material for domestic phosphoric acid and single super phosphate (SSP) manufacturing; imports phosphoric acid (primarily from Morocco and Jordan) as an intermediate for DAP production in India; and imports finished DAP (primarily from Saudi Arabia, China, and Morocco) when domestic manufacturing cannot meet demand.
Morocco alone holds approximately 70–75% of the world’s known phosphate reserves. Jordan holds much of the rest. This geographic concentration of a strategically critical nutrient in two countries — neither of which is fully stable politically — represents a long-run vulnerability for Indian agriculture. India’s phosphate diplomacy with Morocco’s OCP Group (the world’s largest phosphate company) and Jordan’s JPMC is consequently of significant strategic importance, even if it rarely makes headlines.


Potassium (MOP): The Most Vulnerable Nutrient
Muriate of Potash (MOP) is India’s most exposed fertilizer import. India produces essentially zero potassium domestically. Every kilogramme of MOP applied to Indian fields — and India uses approximately 4–5 MT per year — must be imported. The sourcing geography has historically been dominated by Belarus (Belaruskali, one of the world’s largest potash producers), Canada (Nutrien, formerly Potash Corp), and Russia (Uralkali).
The 2022 geopolitical crisis turned this vulnerability into a crisis. Western sanctions on Belarus, imposed in response to the Lukashenko government’s political repression, cut off Belaruskali exports from the global financial system. Russia’s invasion of Ukraine triggered broader commodity disruption. The result was a near-tripling of MOP prices in 2021–22, from approximately USD 200/MT to over USD 600/MT. India’s government-to-government diplomacy — including a controversial direct deal with Belaruskali negotiated outside the formal sanctions framework — provided some relief, but the episode exposed the complete absence of strategic potash reserves or supply alternatives.

title: India’s Fertilizer Dependency: Geopolitical Risks, Price Shocks, and the Subsidy Burden
section: The Price Shock of 2021–22: A Fiscal Emergency
text:
The Price Shock of 2021–22: A Fiscal Emergency
The fertilizer price crisis of 2021–22 was the most severe in India’s post-Independence agricultural history. The international price of urea, which had been USD 250–300/MT for much of 2019–20, crossed USD 900/MT at its peak in late 2021. DAP prices, which were USD 300–350/MT in 2020, reached USD 900–1,000/MT. MOP prices tripled from USD 200/MT to over USD 600/MT.
In normal years, India’s fertilizer subsidy bill runs to approximately ₹70,000–80,000 crore. In 2021–22, it crossed ₹2.5 lakh crore — more than three times the normal level — as the government absorbed the price differential between international prices and the controlled retail prices paid by farmers. This extraordinary expenditure crowded out other budgetary priorities and strained the fiscal deficit targets.
India’s fertilizer pricing architecture creates this fiscal risk structurally. Retail fertilizer prices — particularly for urea — are fixed by the government and have not been revised upward meaningfully in over a decade. The “Maximum Retail Price” of urea is approximately ₹5.36 per kg, while its economic cost (at 2021–22 international prices) was approximately ₹30–35 per kg. The entire gap is borne by the government through the fertilizer subsidy scheme. This system, while protecting farmers from price volatility in the short run, creates perverse incentives — excessive urea use (India over-applies urea relative to phosphorus and potassium, distorting the N:P:K ratio), soil degradation, and groundwater contamination — while also creating a massive, unhedged fiscal exposure to global commodity markets.

title: India’s Fertilizer Dependency: Geopolitical Risks, Price Shocks, and the Subsidy Burden
section: Structural Reform: What India Needs to Do
text:
Structural Reform: What India Needs to Do
Addressing India’s fertilizer vulnerability requires action on multiple fronts.
Expanding domestic urea capacity is the first priority. The completed revival of the five sick urea plants has been helpful, but the pipeline needs to continue. New urea plants, particularly using coal-to-urea technology in coal-rich states like Jharkhand and Odisha, could reduce import dependence further — though this comes at the cost of higher carbon emissions and may conflict with India’s climate commitments.
Developing strategic fertilizer reserves is urgently needed. China maintains a 6–9 month fertilizer reserve; India holds barely a month’s worth of buffer stock. Creating a 3-month reserve of all three key fertilizers (urea, DAP, MOP) would provide a meaningful buffer against supply shocks and price spikes, reducing the pressure to accept unfavourable terms in crisis procurement.
Diversifying potash sourcing requires diplomatic investment. India needs to engage more deeply with Canadian producers, explore the viability of Jordanian potash, and develop relationships with emerging potash producers in Ethiopia and Congo. The government’s agricultural attaché offices at key embassies should be working proactively on this.
Reforming the urea subsidy architecture is the most politically difficult but most important structural change. Transitioning from a fixed-price subsidy to a direct benefit transfer (DBT) system — where farmers receive cash support and buy fertilizers at market prices — would eliminate the perverse incentives created by the current system, reduce over-application of urea, and decouple the government’s fiscal exposure from international price volatility. The Neem Coating of Urea initiative (introduced in 2015 to slow urea dissolution and reduce diversion) was a modest first step; the broader pricing reform remains unfinished business.
Investing in fertilizer-efficient crop varieties and soil health can reduce the absolute quantity of fertilizer needed. India’s fertilizer use efficiency — the kilograms of additional crop output per kilogram of nutrient applied — is well below global best practice. Better soil testing, precision application technologies, and the promotion of organic/bio-fertilizer supplementation can improve efficiency without reducing yields, gradually reducing the volume of imports required.

title: India’s Fertilizer Dependency: Geopolitical Risks, Price Shocks, and the Subsidy Burden
section: The Geopolitical Dimension: Fertilizers as a National Security Issue
text:
The Geopolitical Dimension: Fertilizers as a National Security Issue
The fertilizer crisis of 2021–22 should be viewed not merely as a commodity market event but as a national security stress test. Food production depends on fertilizers; if fertilizer supplies are cut off or priced out of reach for an extended period, Indian crop yields would fall, food prices would surge, and rural livelihoods would be devastated.
India’s geopolitical situation — with complex relationships with China (historically its largest urea supplier), Russia and Belarus (potash), and the Middle East/North Africa (phosphates and DAP) — means that fertilizer procurement is inescapably entangled with foreign policy. The government has shown pragmatism in this regard: India did not join the Western sanctions against Belarus or Russia and continued to negotiate fertilizer supplies through government-to-government channels. This realpolitik approach has helped manage the immediate supply crisis but does not address the structural dependency.
A deeper strategic investment in domestic production capacity, alternative supply relationships, and demand-side efficiency improvements is the only durable solution.

title: India’s Fertilizer Dependency: Geopolitical Risks, Price Shocks, and the Subsidy Burden
text:
Conclusion
India’s fertilizer import dependency is a complex web of geological constraints (zero potash and limited phosphate reserves), policy distortions (urea subsidies that encourage over-use), and geopolitical exposures (concentration in a few supplier countries). The 2021–22 price crisis was the most expensive signal yet that this architecture is unsustainable. Reforming it requires political courage — particularly around urea pricing — as well as sustained investment in domestic manufacturing, strategic reserves, and soil health. The alternative — waiting for the next price shock to impose even greater fiscal and food security costs — is not an option that India’s agriculture, economy, or 700 million farming-dependent citizens can afford.

Data sources: Department of Fertilisers, Ministry of Chemicals; Ministry of Commerce (import statistics); World Bank Fertilizer Price database; IFA (International Fertilizer Association). Analysis by Pawan.
