title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
India’s merchandise trade has grown from under USD 200 billion (2005) to over USD 1.1 trillion (2023), making it the world’s 10th largest merchandise trading nation. This analysis examines total export and import trends, the trade balance trajectory, and the composition of key sectors — cereals, fertilizers, petroleum, and edible oils — using Harmonised System (HS) code classification.







TipKey Findings at a Glance




📈 India’s exports crossed USD 450 billion in 2022–23, up from ~USD 170 billion a decade earlier
📉 Trade deficit is structural and widening: typically USD 200–250 billion annually in recent years
🌾 Rice dominates cereal exports: India is the world’s largest rice exporter, accounting for ~40% of global trade
⛽ Petroleum products are India’s top export category — a testament to refinery capacity expansions
🌱 Fertilizer imports are large and import-intensive: India imports ~$10 billion worth of fertilizers annually
🔄 Export coverage ratio has declined from ~70% (early 2000s) to ~55% (recent years), indicating growing import dependence

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
1 Setup


Code
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggiraph)
library(readxl)
library(viridis)
library(scales)

my_theme <- theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    text             = element_text(color = "#2c3e50"),
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5,
                                    margin = margin(8, 0, 8, 0)),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5, color = "#7f8c8d"),
    plot.caption     = element_text(size = 9, color = "#95a5a6", hjust = 0),
    axis.title       = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    axis.text        = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1),
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    legend.title     = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "#ecf0f1", linewidth = 0.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "#f8f9fa", color = NA),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

std_opts <- list(
  opts_tooltip(
    css = paste0("background-color:#2c3e50;color:white;padding:8px 12px;",
                 "border-radius:6px;font-size:12px;line-height:1.5;")
  ),
  opts_hover(css = "stroke-width:2.5;opacity:1;"),
  opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;"),
  opts_zoom(min = 0.5, max = 3),
  opts_toolbar(position = "topright"),
  opts_sizing(rescale = TRUE, width = 1)
)

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
2 Data Loading & Cleaning


Code
usd <- as.data.table(readRDS("/home/pawan/trade/totaltrade/total_exportsusd.rds"))
qty <- as.data.table(readRDS("/home/pawan/trade/totaltrade/total_exportsqty.rds"))
musd <- as.data.table(readRDS("/home/pawan/trade/totaltrade/total_importsusd.rds"))
mqty <- as.data.table(readRDS("/home/pawan/trade/totaltrade/total_importsqty.rds"))
twodigit <- as.data.table(read_excel("/home/pawan/trade/totaltrade/two-digit.xlsx"))

## Standardised cleaning function
clean_trade <- function(dt, val_col) {
  dt[, `:=`(HSCode2 = substr(HSCode, 1, 2),
            HSCode4 = substr(HSCode, 1, 4),
            HSCode6 = substr(HSCode, 1, 6))]
  dt[, (val_col) := as.numeric(gsub(",", "", get(val_col)))]
  dt[is.na(get(val_col)), (val_col) := 0]
  dt
}

usd  <- clean_trade(usd,  "Export")
qty  <- clean_trade(qty,  "Export")
musd <- clean_trade(musd, "Import")
mqty <- clean_trade(mqty, "Import")

## Merge HS classification
twodigit[, HSCode2 := as.numeric(HSCode2)]
qty[,  HSCode2 := as.numeric(HSCode2)]
mqty[, HSCode2 := as.numeric(HSCode2)]
qty  <- merge(qty,  twodigit, by = "HSCode2", all.x = TRUE)
mqty <- merge(mqty, twodigit, by = "HSCode2", all.x = TRUE)

## Aggregate totals
export_usd <- usd[,  .(Export_USD = sum(Export, na.rm = TRUE)), by = Year]
import_usd <- musd[, .(Import_USD = sum(Import, na.rm = TRUE)), by = Year]
export_qty <- qty[,  .(Export_Qty = sum(Export, na.rm = TRUE)), by = Year]
import_qty <- mqty[, .(Import_Qty = sum(Import, na.rm = TRUE)), by = Year]

trade <- Reduce(function(a, b) merge(a, b, by = "Year"),
                list(export_usd, import_usd, export_qty, import_qty))
trade[, `:=`(
  Trade_Balance   = Export_USD - Import_USD,
  Trade_Volume    = Export_USD + Import_USD,
  Export_Coverage = round(Export_USD * 100 / Import_USD, 1),
  Export_Growth   = round((Export_USD / shift(Export_USD) - 1) * 100, 1),
  Import_Growth   = round((Import_USD / shift(Import_USD) - 1) * 100, 1)
)]

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
3 Trade Balance Trend


Code
trade_long <- melt(trade[, .(Year, Export_USD, Import_USD)],
                   id = "Year", variable.name = "Flow", value.name = "USD")
trade_long[, tip := paste0("<b>", ifelse(Flow == "Export_USD", "Exports", "Imports"), "</b><br>",
                           "Year: ", Year, "<br>",
                           "Value: USD <b>", format(round(USD / 1000), big.mark = ","), "</b> Bn")]

pt <- ggplot() +
  ## Trade balance area
  geom_area(data = trade, aes(x = Year, y = Trade_Balance / 1000, group = 1),
            fill = "#e74c3c", alpha = 0.15) +
  ## Export & import lines
  geom_line_interactive(
    data = trade_long,
    aes(x = Year, y = USD / 1000, color = Flow, tooltip = tip,
        data_id = paste(Flow, Year), group = Flow),
    linewidth = 1.3
  ) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    data = trade_long,
    aes(x = Year, y = USD / 1000, color = Flow, tooltip = tip,
        data_id = paste(Flow, Year)),
    size = 2.5
  ) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "#7f8c8d") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Export_USD" = "#27ae60", "Import_USD" = "#e74c3c"),
                     labels = c("Export_USD" = "Exports", "Import_USD" = "Imports")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar(suffix = "B", scale = 1)) +
  labs(title   = "India's Total Merchandise Trade",
       subtitle = "Exports, imports, and trade deficit (USD Billions)",
       x = "Year", y = "USD Billions",
       caption = "Source: Ministry of Commerce, India | Analysis: Pawan") +
  my_theme

girafe(ggobj = pt, width_svg = 11, height_svg = 6, options = std_opts)







3.1 Export Coverage Ratio


Code
trade[, tip_cov := paste0("Year: ", Year, "<br>",
                          "Export coverage: <b>", Export_Coverage, "%</b><br>",
                          "(Exports ÷ Imports × 100)")]
pc <- ggplot(trade[!is.na(Export_Coverage)],
             aes(x = Year, y = Export_Coverage, tooltip = tip_cov, data_id = Year, group = 1)) +
  geom_col_interactive(
    aes(fill = Export_Coverage < 60),
    width = 0.75, alpha = 0.85
  ) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed", color = "#27ae60", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 60,  linetype = "dotted", color = "#e74c3c",  linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#3498db", "TRUE" = "#e67e22"), guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(scale = 1)) +
  labs(title   = "Export Coverage Ratio",
       subtitle = "How much of India's imports are covered by export earnings?",
       x = "Year", y = "Exports as % of Imports",
       caption = "Green dashed = 100% (balanced trade); Red dotted = 60% threshold") +
  my_theme

girafe(ggobj = pc, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 5, options = std_opts)

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
4 Cereal Exports: Composition
India is the world’s largest rice exporter and a significant wheat exporter in surplus years. The 2022–23 wheat export ban reflects India’s policy of managing domestic food price inflation.


Code
cereals <- qty[HSCode2 == 10]
cereals[, Commodity := fcase(
  HSCode4 == "1006", "Rice",
  HSCode4 == "1001", "Wheat",
  HSCode4 == "1005", "Maize",
  HSCode4 == "1007", "Grain Sorghum",
  HSCode4 == "1008", "Millet & Buckwheat",
  default = "Other Cereals"
)]
cereals_yr <- cereals[, .(Qty = sum(Export, na.rm = TRUE)), by = .(Year, Commodity)]
cereals_yr[, Total := sum(Qty), by = Year]
cereals_yr[, Share := round(Qty * 100 / Total)]
cereals_yr[, Commodity := factor(Commodity,
  levels = c("Other Cereals", "Millet & Buckwheat", "Grain Sorghum",
             "Maize", "Wheat", "Rice"))]
cereals_yr[, tip := paste0("<b>", Commodity, "</b><br>",
                           "Year: ", Year, "<br>",
                           "Qty: ", format(round(Qty), big.mark = ","), " '000T<br>",
                           "Share: ", Share, "%")]

pce <- ggplot(cereals_yr, aes(x = Year, y = Qty, fill = Commodity,
                               tooltip = tip, data_id = paste(Commodity, Year))) +
  geom_bar_interactive(stat = "identity", position = "stack", width = 0.75, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(Share >= 8, paste0(Share, "%"), "")),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 2.8,
            color = "white", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_viridis(discrete = TRUE, option = "D") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma_format()) +
  labs(title   = "Cereal Exports from India by Commodity",
       subtitle = "Rice dominates; wheat exports vary by policy and domestic supply conditions",
       x = "Year", y = "Quantity ('000 Tonnes)",
       caption = "Source: Ministry of Commerce | Analysis: Pawan") +
  my_theme

girafe(ggobj = pce, width_svg = 11, height_svg = 6, options = std_opts)

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
5 Fertilizer Imports: Composition


Code
fert <- mqty[HSCode2 == 31]
fert[, Commodity := fcase(
  HSCode4 == "3102", "Nitrogenous (Urea & Others)",
  HSCode4 == "3104", "Potassic (MOP)",
  HSCode4 == "3105", "DAP & NPK Compounds",
  default = "Other Fertilizers"
)]
fert_yr <- fert[, .(Qty = sum(Import, na.rm = TRUE)), by = .(Year, Commodity)]
fert_yr[, Total := sum(Qty), by = Year]
fert_yr[, Share := round(Qty * 100 / Total)]
fert_yr[, tip := paste0("<b>", Commodity, "</b><br>",
                        "Year: ", Year, "<br>",
                        "Qty: ", format(round(Qty), big.mark = ","), " '000T<br>",
                        "Share: ", Share, "%")]

pfe <- ggplot(fert_yr, aes(x = Year, y = Qty, fill = Commodity,
                            tooltip = tip, data_id = paste(Commodity, Year))) +
  geom_bar_interactive(stat = "identity", position = "stack", width = 0.75, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(Share >= 8, paste0(Share, "%"), "")),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 2.8,
            color = "white", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_viridis(discrete = TRUE, option = "C") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma_format()) +
  labs(title   = "Fertilizer Imports by Type",
       subtitle = "Nitrogenous (urea-based) fertilizers dominate imports by volume",
       x = "Year", y = "Quantity ('000 Tonnes)",
       caption = "Source: Ministry of Commerce | Analysis: Pawan") +
  my_theme

girafe(ggobj = pfe, width_svg = 11, height_svg = 6, options = std_opts)

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
6 Petroleum Imports: Composition


Code
petro <- mqty[HSCode2 == 27]
petro[, Commodity := fcase(
  HSCode4 == "2701", "Coal",
  HSCode4 == "2709", "Crude Petroleum Oils",
  HSCode4 == "2711", "Petroleum Gases (LNG/LPG)",
  default = "Other Mineral Fuels"
)]
petro_yr <- petro[, .(Qty = sum(Import, na.rm = TRUE)), by = .(Year, Commodity)]
petro_yr[, Total := sum(Qty), by = Year]
petro_yr[, Share := round(Qty * 100 / Total)]
petro_yr[, tip := paste0("<b>", Commodity, "</b><br>",
                         "Year: ", Year, "<br>",
                         "Qty: ", format(round(Qty), big.mark = ","), " '000T<br>",
                         "Share: ", Share, "%")]

ppe <- ggplot(petro_yr, aes(x = Year, y = Qty, fill = Commodity,
                             tooltip = tip, data_id = paste(Commodity, Year))) +
  geom_bar_interactive(stat = "identity", position = "stack", width = 0.75, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(Share >= 8, paste0(Share, "%"), "")),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 2.8,
            color = "white", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Crude Petroleum Oils"    = "#e67e22",
                               "Coal"                     = "#2c3e50",
                               "Petroleum Gases (LNG/LPG)" = "#8e44ad",
                               "Other Mineral Fuels"       = "#bdc3c7")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma_format()) +
  labs(title   = "Petroleum & Mineral Fuel Imports by Type",
       subtitle = "Crude petroleum dominates; LNG imports rising as gas infrastructure expands",
       x = "Year", y = "Quantity ('000 Tonnes)",
       caption = "Source: Ministry of Commerce | Analysis: Pawan") +
  my_theme

girafe(ggobj = ppe, width_svg = 11, height_svg = 6, options = std_opts)

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
7 Edible Oils & Fats Import Volume


Code
oils <- mqty[HSCode2 == 15]
oils_yr <- oils[, .(Qty = sum(Import, na.rm = TRUE)), by = Year]
oils_yr[, tip := paste0("Year: <b>", Year, "</b><br>",
                        "Edible oils import: ",
                        format(round(Qty / 1000), big.mark = ","), " '000 Tonnes")]

po <- ggplot(oils_yr, aes(x = Year, y = Qty / 1000, tooltip = tip, data_id = Year, group = 1)) +
  geom_area_interactive(fill = "#f39c12", alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line_interactive(linewidth = 1.2, color = "#d68910") +
  geom_point_interactive(size = 2.5, color = "#b7770d") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma_format(suffix = " '000T")) +
  labs(title   = "Vegetable Fats & Edible Oils Import Volume",
       subtitle = "Annual import trend — HS Chapter 15",
       x = "Year", y = "Quantity ('000 Tonnes)",
       caption = "Source: Ministry of Commerce | Analysis: Pawan") +
  my_theme

girafe(ggobj = po, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 5, options = std_opts)

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
8 Key Takeaways






ImportantSummary of Findings




India’s trade deficit is deep and structural: Petroleum, fertilizers, and edible oils together account for the bulk of the import bill, and all three are heavily import-dependent by structural necessity.
Cereal exports are policy-sensitive: The government alternates between encouraging exports (when there is surplus) and banning them (when domestic prices rise) — illustrating the tension between farmer welfare and consumer welfare.
India is the world’s largest rice exporter: Rice contributes the bulk of cereal export earnings, though the recent quality and residue concerns in some markets require attention.
Petroleum imports are the largest single category: India’s energy import dependence (~85% crude oil from overseas) is both an economic vulnerability and a geopolitical consideration.
The export coverage ratio declining toward 55% suggests imports are growing faster than exports — a medium-term current account concern.
Fertilizer import dependence reinforces the need to expand domestic production capacity and reduce over-subsidisation, which incentivises excessive fertilizer use and soil degradation.

title: India’s Fertilizer Import & Production Statistics
text:
India is one of the world’s largest consumers and importers of chemical fertilizers. With its cultivated area of ~140 million hectares and a growing population, ensuring a steady supply of nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K) is a matter of national food security. This analysis covers urea, DAP (diammonium phosphate), MOP (muriate of potash), rock phosphate, and phosphoric acid imports from 2016 onward — examining volumes, source diversification, international price dynamics, and domestic production capacity.







TipKey Findings at a Glance




🌾 Urea dominates nitrogen supply: India imports 6–9 million tonnes annually, with China historically the top supplier
🌍 Morocco controls phosphate chain: Jordan, Morocco, and Saudi Arabia together supply most of India’s DAP and rock phosphate
💊 MOP is the least diversified: Belarus, Canada, and Russia account for the vast majority of potash imports
📈 Price shocks in 2021–22: Urea, DAP, and MOP prices each more than doubled during the Russia–Ukraine commodity crisis
🏭 Domestic urea production is near capacity ceiling: India has expanded urea manufacturing, but imports remain essential
⚖️ DAP production lags demand: India imports 3–4 MT of DAP annually despite having some domestic capacity

title: India’s Fertilizer Import & Production Statistics
text:
India is one of the world’s largest consumers and importers of chemical fertilizers. With its cultivated area of ~140 million hectares and a growing population, ensuring a steady supply of nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K) is a matter of national food security. This analysis covers urea, DAP (diammonium phosphate), MOP (muriate of potash), rock phosphate, and phosphoric acid imports from 2016 onward — examining volumes, source diversification, international price dynamics, and domestic production capacity.







TipKey Findings at a Glance




🌾 Urea dominates nitrogen supply: India imports 6–9 million tonnes annually, with China historically the top supplier
🌍 Morocco controls phosphate chain: Jordan, Morocco, and Saudi Arabia together supply most of India’s DAP and rock phosphate
💊 MOP is the least diversified: Belarus, Canada, and Russia account for the vast majority of potash imports
📈 Price shocks in 2021–22: Urea, DAP, and MOP prices each more than doubled during the Russia–Ukraine commodity crisis
🏭 Domestic urea production is near capacity ceiling: India has expanded urea manufacturing, but imports remain essential
⚖️ DAP production lags demand: India imports 3–4 MT of DAP annually despite having some domestic capacity

title: India’s Fertilizer Import & Production Statistics
text:
1 Setup


Code
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggiraph)
library(scales)
library(tidyr)
library(viridis)

options(scipen = 99999)

## ── Unified theme ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
my_theme <- theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    text             = element_text(color = "#2c3e50"),
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5,
                                    margin = margin(8, 0, 8, 0)),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5, color = "#7f8c8d"),
    plot.caption     = element_text(size = 9, color = "#95a5a6", hjust = 0),
    axis.title       = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    axis.text        = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1),
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    legend.title     = element_blank(),
    legend.text      = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "#ecf0f1", linewidth = 0.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "#f8f9fa", color = NA),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11)
  )

## ── Standard girafe options ───────────────────────────────────────────────
std_opts <- list(
  opts_tooltip(
    css = paste0("background-color:#2c3e50;color:white;padding:8px 12px;",
                 "border-radius:6px;font-size:12px;line-height:1.5;"),
    use_fill = FALSE
  ),
  opts_hover(css = "fill-opacity:0.85;stroke:white;stroke-width:1.5;"),
  opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.25;"),
  opts_zoom(min = 0.5, max = 3),
  opts_toolbar(position = "topright"),
  opts_sizing(rescale = TRUE, width = 1)
)

## ── Reusable interactive stacked bar ──────────────────────────────────────
fert_bar <- function(data, title, subtitle, palette_opt = "D") {
  data <- as.data.table(data)
  data[, tip := paste0("<b>", Country, "</b><br>",
                       "Year: ", Year, "<br>",
                       "Quantity: ", round(Qty, 2), " MMT<br>",
                       "Share: ", Percent, "%")]

  p <- ggplot(data, aes(x = Year, y = Qty, fill = Country,
                        tooltip = tip,
                        data_id = paste(Country, Year))) +
    geom_bar_interactive(stat = "identity", position = "stack",
                         width = 0.75, alpha = 0.9) +
    geom_text(
      aes(label = ifelse(Percent >= 8, paste0(Percent, "%"), "")),
      position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
      size = 3, color = "white", fontface = "bold"
    ) +
    scale_fill_viridis(discrete = TRUE, option = palette_opt,
                       begin = 0.1, end = 0.9) +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = comma_format()) +
    labs(title = title, subtitle = subtitle,
         y = "Quantity (Million Metric Tonnes)",
         x = "Year",
         caption = "Source: Ministry of Commerce, India | Analysis: Pawan") +
    my_theme

  girafe(ggobj = p, width_svg = 11, height_svg = 6, options = std_opts)
}

title: India’s Fertilizer Import & Production Statistics
text:
2 Data Loading and Preparation


Code
## Load raw import data
t1 <- readRDS("/home/pawan/fertilizer/urea_importqty.rds")
t2 <- readRDS("/home/pawan/fertilizer/dap_importqty.rds")
t3 <- readRDS("/home/pawan/fertilizer/rock_phosphate_importqty.rds")
t4 <- readRDS("/home/pawan/fertilizer/phosphoric_acid_importqty.rds")
t5 <- readRDS("/home/pawan/fertilizer/potash_importqty.rds")

## Clean commas from quantity column
for (d in list(t1, t2, t3, t4, t5)) {
  d[, ImportMonth := gsub(",", "", ImportMonth)]
}

## Helper: filter to country-level, aggregate, group, and calculate shares
prep_fert <- function(dt, major_countries, country_renames = NULL,
                      year_cutoff = "2015") {
  ## Rename countries if needed
  if (!is.null(country_renames)) {
    for (i in seq_along(country_renames)) {
      dt[Country == names(country_renames)[i], Country := country_renames[i]]
    }
  }
  out <- dt[Country != "Total",
            .(Qty = sum(as.numeric(ImportMonth), na.rm = TRUE)),
            by = .(Country, Year)]
  ## Group minor countries
  out[!Country %in% major_countries, Country := "OTHER COUNTRY"]
  out <- out[, .(Qty = sum(Qty)), by = .(Country, Year)]
  out[, Qty := Qty / 100000]   ## tonnes → million metric tonnes
  out <- out[Year > year_cutoff]
  out[, Percent := round(Qty * 100 / sum(Qty)), by = Year]
  out
}

## ── Urea ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
urea <- prep_fert(
  t1,
  major_countries  = c("CHINA", "OMAN", "IRAN", "UKRAINE", "EGYPT", "RUSSIA", "INDONESIA"),
  country_renames  = c("CHINA P RP" = "CHINA", "EGYPT A RP" = "EGYPT")
)
urea[, Country := factor(Country,
  levels = c("OTHER COUNTRY","INDONESIA","EGYPT","RUSSIA","UKRAINE","OMAN","IRAN","CHINA"))]

## ── DAP ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
dap <- prep_fert(
  t2,
  major_countries = c("CHINA", "SAUDI ARAB", "MOROCCO", "JORDAN", "RUSSIA"),
  country_renames = c("CHINA P RP" = "CHINA")
)
dap[, Country := factor(Country,
  levels = c("OTHER COUNTRY","MOROCCO","JORDAN","RUSSIA","CHINA","SAUDI ARAB"))]

## ── Rock Phosphate ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
rockp <- prep_fert(
  t3,
  major_countries = c("EGYPT", "JORDAN", "MOROCCO", "PERU", "TOGO"),
  country_renames = c("EGYPT A RP" = "EGYPT")
)
rockp[, Country := factor(Country,
  levels = c("OTHER COUNTRY","PERU","TOGO","EGYPT","MOROCCO","JORDAN"))]

## ── Phosphoric Acid ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
phosacid <- prep_fert(
  t4,
  major_countries = c("MOROCCO", "JORDAN", "SENEGAL")
)
phosacid[, Country := factor(Country,
  levels = c("OTHER COUNTRY","SENEGAL","JORDAN","MOROCCO"))]

## ── MOP ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
mop <- prep_fert(
  t5,
  major_countries = c("CANADA","BELARUS","ISRAEL","JORDAN","LITHUANIA","RUSSIA")
)
mop[, Country := factor(Country,
  levels = c("OTHER COUNTRY","RUSSIA","LITHUANIA","JORDAN","ISRAEL","BELARUS","CANADA"))]

title: India’s Fertilizer Import & Production Statistics
text:
3 Urea Imports
Urea is the workhorse of India’s nitrogen fertilizer sector. China was historically the dominant supplier, but geopolitical shifts and India’s efforts to diversify have brought Oman, Iran, and Egypt into greater prominence.


Code
fert_bar(urea,
  title    = "India's Urea Imports by Source Country",
  subtitle = "Annual import volumes 2016–2024 (Million Metric Tonnes)",
  palette_opt = "D"
)












NoteUrea Supply Geography



China’s dominance as a urea supplier to India peaked around 2016–17. Since then, India has strategically diversified toward Oman (Oman India Fertiliser Company — OMIFCO, a joint venture), Iran, and Russia. This diversification provides some buffer against Chinese export restrictions, which Beijing has imposed intermittently.

title: India’s Fertilizer Import & Production Statistics
text:
4 DAP Imports
DAP (Di-Ammonium Phosphate) provides both nitrogen and phosphorus to crops. India’s domestic DAP production covers only ~40% of demand, necessitating large-scale imports.


Code
fert_bar(dap,
  title    = "India's DAP Imports by Source Country",
  subtitle = "Saudi Arabia and China are the primary suppliers; Morocco is rising",
  palette_opt = "C"
)

title: India’s Fertilizer Import & Production Statistics
text:
5 Rock Phosphate Imports
Rock phosphate is the primary raw material for manufacturing phosphatic fertilizers domestically. Jordan and Morocco collectively control a large fraction of global reserves.


Code
fert_bar(rockp,
  title    = "India's Rock Phosphate Imports by Source Country",
  subtitle = "Jordan and Morocco supply the bulk of India's rock phosphate needs",
  palette_opt = "E"
)

title: India’s Fertilizer Import & Production Statistics
text:
6 Phosphoric Acid Imports
Phosphoric acid is a key intermediate in DAP and SSP (Single Super Phosphate) production. Morocco’s dominance here makes India’s fertilizer manufacturing sector highly dependent on North African phosphate geology.


Code
fert_bar(phosacid,
  title    = "India's Phosphoric Acid Imports by Source Country",
  subtitle = "Morocco supplies the majority of India's phosphoric acid imports",
  palette_opt = "B"
)

title: India’s Fertilizer Import & Production Statistics
text:
7 MOP (Muriate of Potash) Imports
India imports nearly all its potash requirement. The 2022 Belarus sanctions and Russia–Ukraine war caused a severe MOP supply squeeze globally — India had to negotiate emergency supplies directly with Canada and Russia.


Code
fert_bar(mop,
  title    = "India's MOP (Potash) Imports by Source Country",
  subtitle = "Canada, Belarus, and Russia dominate; Belarus sanctions caused supply disruption in 2022",
  palette_opt = "A"
)

title: India’s Fertilizer Import & Production Statistics
text:
8 International Fertilizer Prices
The 2021–22 commodity supercycle caused Urea, DAP, and MOP prices to spike to multi-decade highs. This dramatically increased India’s fertilizer subsidy bill and led to supply disruptions.


Code
price <- fread("/home/pawan/fertilizer/international_fert_price.csv")
price[, Year  := paste0("20", Year)]
price[, Month := substr(Month, 1, 3)]
price[, Date  := as.Date(paste0(Year, "-", Month, "-20"), format = "%Y-%b-%d")]

price_long <- melt(price, id.vars = c("Month", "Year", "Date"))
price_long  <- price_long[variable %in% c("Urea", "DAP", "MOP")]
price_long[, tip := paste0("<b>", variable, "</b><br>",
                           "Date: ", format(Date, "%b %Y"), "<br>",
                           "Price: USD <b>", round(value, 0), "</b>/MT")]

pp <- ggplot(price_long, aes(x = Date, y = value, color = variable,
                              tooltip = tip, data_id = paste(variable, Date),
                              group = variable)) +
  geom_line_interactive(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point_interactive(size = 1.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +
  scale_color_viridis(discrete = TRUE, option = "D") +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%b-%y", date_breaks = "6 months") +
  labs(title   = "International Fertilizer Prices",
       subtitle = "Monthly USD/MT prices for Urea, DAP, and MOP",
       x = "Month", y = "Price (USD/MT)",
       caption = "Source: World Bank / IFA | Analysis: Pawan") +
  my_theme +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

girafe(ggobj = pp, width_svg = 11, height_svg = 9, options = std_opts)












WarningPrice Shock Impact on India



India’s fertilizer subsidy expenditure crossed ₹2.5 lakh crore in 2021–22, driven almost entirely by the global price surge. Since India offers subsidised fertilizers to farmers at controlled retail prices, global price increases are absorbed by the government — creating enormous fiscal pressure.

title: India’s Fertilizer Import & Production Statistics
text:
9 Domestic Production: Urea & DAP


Code
fert <- readRDS("/home/pawan/fertilizer/production-import/fert_production_import.RDS")
fert <- fert[Year != "Total"]
fert[, Date := as.Date(paste(Year, "-", Month, "-15", sep = ""), "%Y-%b-%d")]
fert_long <- melt(fert, id = c("Year", "Month", "Date"))
fert_long <- fert_long[variable != "SNo" & variable != "Blank"]
fert_long <- separate(as.data.frame(fert_long), variable,
                       into = c("Fertilizer", "Variable"), sep = "_")
setDT(fert_long)

fert1 <- fread("/home/pawan/fertilizer/production-import.csv")
fert1[, Date := as.Date(paste(Year, "-", Month, "-15", sep = ""), "%Y-%b-%d")]
fert1_long <- melt(fert1, id = c("Year", "Month", "Date", "Fertilizer"))
fert1_long[variable == "Production_Achievement", variable := "Production"]

## Combine and clean
prod_old  <- fert_long[Variable == "Production",
                       .(Year = as.numeric(Year), Month, Date, Fertilizer, value)]
prod_new  <- fert1_long[variable == "Production",
                        .(Year = as.numeric(Year), Month, Date, Fertilizer, value)]

production <- rbind(prod_old[Year < 2018], prod_new)
production <- production[Fertilizer %in% c("Urea", "DAP") & !is.na(value)]
production[, value := value / 100000]  ## → Million Metric Tonnes

annual_prod <- production[Year != 2025,
                          .(Qty = sum(value)), by = .(Year, Fertilizer)]
annual_prod[, tip := paste0("<b>", Fertilizer, "</b><br>",
                            "Year: ", Year, "<br>",
                            "Production: ", round(Qty, 2), " MMT")]

pp2 <- ggplot(annual_prod, aes(x = Year, y = Qty, color = Fertilizer,
                                tooltip = tip, data_id = paste(Fertilizer, Year),
                                group = Fertilizer)) +
  geom_line_interactive(linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_point_interactive(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ Fertilizer, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +
  scale_color_viridis(discrete = TRUE, option = "C") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1990, 2025, 2)) +
  labs(title   = "Urea and DAP Production in India",
       subtitle = "Annual domestic production volumes — Million Metric Tonnes",
       x = "Year", y = "Quantity (MMT)",
       caption = "Source: Department of Fertilisers | Analysis: Pawan") +
  my_theme +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

girafe(ggobj = pp2, width_svg = 11, height_svg = 7, options = std_opts)

title: India’s Fertilizer Import & Production Statistics
text:
10 Key Takeaways






ImportantSummary of Findings




India’s fertilizer import dependence is structural: Domestic production covers demand for urea, but phosphate and potash inputs are almost entirely imported.
Supply concentration is a critical vulnerability: 2–3 countries control 80–90% of each fertilizer category’s supply to India. Export restrictions or geopolitical crises in any of these countries cause immediate supply and price shocks.
The 2021–22 global price spike was the most severe fertilizer price event in decades, more than doubling DAP and MOP prices and pushing India’s subsidy bill to record levels.
China’s urea export restrictions in 2021–22 forced India to diversify urea sourcing to Oman, Iran, Egypt, and Russia — a positive structural change, albeit forced.
Potash is the most vulnerable input: India has zero domestic reserves of potassium and relies entirely on imports — primarily from Belarus, Canada, and Russia, all subject to geopolitical disruption risk.
Domestic urea capacity has grown significantly since 2014 as several gas-based plants were revived/built, but the country still imports 6–9 MMT annually.

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text: India’s merchandise trade has grown dramatically over the past two decades — exports crossing USD 450 billion in recent years while imports hover around USD 700 billion, producing a persistent and growing trade deficit. This report decomposes the story through HS code level analysis: cereals (HS 10), edible oils (HS 15), fertilizers (HS 31), and petroleum products (HS 27).

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text: India’s merchandise trade has grown dramatically over the past two decades — exports crossing USD 450 billion in recent years while imports hover around USD 700 billion, producing a persistent and growing trade deficit. This report decomposes the story through HS code level analysis: cereals (HS 10), edible oils (HS 15), fertilizers (HS 31), and petroleum products (HS 27).

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
Setup


Code
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggiraph)
library(scales)
library(gt)
library(plotly)
library(viridis)
library(readxl)
library(patchwork)
library(dplyr)

my_theme <- theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, margin = margin(b = 8)),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5, color = "#7F8C8D"),
    plot.caption    = element_text(size = 9, color = "#95A5A6", hjust = 0),
    axis.title      = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    axis.text       = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title    = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major= element_line(color = "#ECF0F1", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor= element_blank(),
    strip.background= element_rect(fill = "#F8F9FA", color = NA),
    strip.text      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11)
  )

## Data cleaning helper
clean_trade <- function(dt, col) {
  dt[, (col) := as.numeric(gsub("[,]","", get(col)))]
  dt[is.na(get(col)), (col) := 0]
  dt[, `:=`(HSCode2=substr(HSCode,1,2),
             HSCode4=substr(HSCode,1,4),
             HSCode6=substr(HSCode,1,6))]
  dt
}

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
Section 1 · Data Loading & Aggregation


Code
usd  <- clean_trade(readRDS("/home/pawan/trade/totaltrade/total_exportsusd.rds"),  "Export")
qty  <- clean_trade(readRDS("/home/pawan/trade/totaltrade/total_exportsqty.rds"),  "Export")
musd <- clean_trade(readRDS("/home/pawan/trade/totaltrade/total_importsusd.rds"),  "Import")
mqty <- clean_trade(readRDS("/home/pawan/trade/totaltrade/total_importsqty.rds"),  "Import")

hs2  <- as.data.table(read_excel("/home/pawan/trade/totaltrade/two-digit.xlsx"))
hs2[, HSCode2 := as.integer(HSCode2)]
qty[,  HSCode2 := as.integer(HSCode2)]
mqty[, HSCode2 := as.integer(HSCode2)]
qty  <- merge(qty,  hs2, by="HSCode2", all.x=TRUE)
mqty <- merge(mqty, hs2, by="HSCode2", all.x=TRUE)

## Annual aggregates
exp_usd  <- usd[,  .(Export_USD  = sum(Export,  na.rm=TRUE)/1000), by=Year]
imp_usd  <- musd[, .(Import_USD  = sum(Import,  na.rm=TRUE)/1000), by=Year]
exp_qty  <- qty[,  .(Export_Qty  = sum(Export,  na.rm=TRUE)), by=Year]
imp_qty  <- mqty[, .(Import_Qty  = sum(Import,  na.rm=TRUE)), by=Year]

trade <- Reduce(function(a,b) merge(a,b,by="Year"),
                list(exp_usd, imp_usd, exp_qty, imp_qty))
trade[, `:=`(
  Balance_USD  = Export_USD - Import_USD,
  Volume_USD   = Export_USD + Import_USD,
  Coverage_Pct = round(Export_USD/Import_USD*100, 1),
  Exp_Growth   = round((Export_USD/shift(Export_USD)-1)*100, 1),
  Imp_Growth   = round((Import_USD/shift(Import_USD)-1)*100, 1)
)]

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
section: Section 2 · Trade Overview: Exports, Imports & Balance
text:
Section 2 · Trade Overview: Exports, Imports & Balance


Code
trade_long <- melt(trade[, .(Year,Export_USD,Import_USD)],
                   id.vars="Year", variable.name="Flow", value.name="Billion_USD")
trade_long[, tt := paste0(Flow,"\nYear: ",Year,"\n$",round(Billion_USD,1),"B")]

p_flows <- ggplot(trade_long, aes(x=Year, y=Billion_USD, color=Flow,
                                   text=tt, group=Flow)) +
  geom_line_interactive(aes(tooltip=tt, data_id=Flow), linewidth=1.3) +
  geom_point(size=2.5, alpha=0.7) +
  geom_ribbon(data=trade, aes(x=Year, ymin=pmin(Export_USD,Import_USD),
                               ymax=pmax(Export_USD,Import_USD),
                               fill=ifelse(Balance_USD<0,"Deficit","Surplus")),
              inherit.aes=FALSE, alpha=0.15) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("Export_USD"="#27AE60","Import_USD"="#E74C3C"),
                     labels=c("Export_USD"="Exports","Import_USD"="Imports")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("Deficit"="#FADBD8","Surplus"="#D5F5E3")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=dollar_format(suffix="B")) +
  my_theme +
  labs(title="India's Merchandise Trade: Exports & Imports",
       subtitle="Shaded area shows trade deficit (red) | Hover for exact values",
       y="USD Billion", x="Year",
       caption="Source: DGCI&S / Ministry of Commerce | Analysis: Pawan")

girafe(ggobj=p_flows, width_svg=12, height_svg=6,
       options=list(
         opts_tooltip(css="background:#fff;color:#333;padding:8px;border-radius:5px;border:1px solid #ddd;"),
         opts_hover(css="stroke-width:2.5;"),
         opts_hover_inv(css="opacity:0.3;"),
         opts_sizing(rescale=TRUE,width=1)))








Code
p_bal <- ggplot(trade, aes(x=Year, y=Balance_USD,
                            fill=Balance_USD>0,
                            text=paste0("Year: ",Year,"\nBalance: $",round(Balance_USD,1),"B"))) +
  geom_col(width=0.75, alpha=0.85) +
  geom_hline(yintercept=0, color="#333", linewidth=0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("TRUE"="#27AE60","FALSE"="#E74C3C"),
                    labels=c("TRUE"="Surplus","FALSE"="Deficit")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=dollar_format(suffix="B")) +
  my_theme +
  labs(title="India's Trade Balance (USD Billion)",
       subtitle="Red = trade deficit | India has run a persistent goods-trade deficit",
       y="Trade Balance (USD Billion)", x="Year",
       caption="Source: DGCI&S | Analysis: Pawan")

ggplotly(p_bal, tooltip="text") |> layout(hovermode="x unified")

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
Section 3 · Cereals Export Composition
India is a major global rice exporter and an intermittent wheat exporter. The composition shifts sharply based on domestic supply and export bans/policy.


Code
cer <- qty[HSCode2 == 10]
cer[, Commodity := fcase(
  HSCode4=="1006", "Rice",
  HSCode4=="1001", "Wheat",
  HSCode4=="1005", "Maize",
  HSCode4=="1008", "Millet/Buckwheat",
  default          = "Other Cereals"
)]
cer_agg <- cer[, .(Qty = sum(Export, na.rm=TRUE)), .(Year, Commodity)]
cer_agg[, Total := sum(Qty), by=Year]
cer_agg[, Share := round(Qty/Total*100)]
cer_agg[, tt    := paste0("<b>",Commodity,"</b><br>Year: ",Year,
                           "<br>Qty: ",format(Qty,big.mark=",")," K Tonnes",
                           "<br>Share: ",Share,"%")]

p_cer <- ggplot(cer_agg, aes(x=Year, y=Qty, fill=Commodity)) +
  geom_bar_interactive(aes(tooltip=tt, data_id=Commodity),
                        stat="identity", position="stack", width=0.75, alpha=0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(label=ifelse(Share<7,"",paste0(Share,"%"))),
            position=position_stack(vjust=0.5), size=2.8, color="white", fontface="bold") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette="Set1") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=comma_format()) +
  my_theme +
  labs(title="India's Cereal Exports by Commodity",
       subtitle="Rice dominates; wheat exports surge in surplus years",
       y="Quantity (000 Tonnes)", x="Year",
       caption="Source: DGCI&S | HS Chapter 10 | Analysis: Pawan")

girafe(ggobj=p_cer, width_svg=12, height_svg=6,
       options=list(
         opts_tooltip(css="background:#fff;color:#333;padding:8px;border-radius:5px;border:1px solid #ddd;"),
         opts_hover(css="opacity:0.85;stroke-width:1.5;"),
         opts_hover_inv(css="opacity:0.3;"),
         opts_sizing(rescale=TRUE,width=1)))

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
Section 4 · Fertilizer Imports


Code
fert <- mqty[HSCode2 == 31]
fert[, Commodity := fcase(
  HSCode4=="3102", "Nitrogenous",
  HSCode4=="3104", "Potassic",
  HSCode4=="3105", "DAP & NPK",
  default          = "Other Chemical Fert"
)]
fert_agg <- fert[, .(Qty = sum(Import, na.rm=TRUE)), .(Year, Commodity)]
fert_agg[, Total := sum(Qty), by=Year]
fert_agg[, Share := round(Qty/Total*100)]
fert_agg[, tt    := paste0("<b>",Commodity,"</b><br>Year: ",Year,
                            "<br>Qty: ",format(Qty,big.mark=",")," K Tonnes",
                            "<br>Share: ",Share,"%")]

p_fert <- ggplot(fert_agg, aes(x=Year, y=Qty, fill=Commodity)) +
  geom_bar_interactive(aes(tooltip=tt, data_id=Commodity),
                        stat="identity", position="stack", width=0.75, alpha=0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(label=ifelse(Share<8,"",paste0(Share,"%"))),
            position=position_stack(vjust=0.5), size=2.8, color="white", fontface="bold") +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option="E") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=comma_format()) +
  my_theme +
  labs(title="India's Fertilizer Imports (HS Chapter 31)",
       subtitle="Nitrogenous (Urea) and DAP/NPK dominate volumes",
       y="Quantity (000 Tonnes)", x="Year",
       caption="Source: DGCI&S | HS Chapter 31 | Analysis: Pawan")

girafe(ggobj=p_fert, width_svg=12, height_svg=6,
       options=list(
         opts_tooltip(css="background:#fff;color:#333;padding:8px;border-radius:5px;border:1px solid #ddd;"),
         opts_hover(css="opacity:0.85;stroke-width:1.5;"),
         opts_sizing(rescale=TRUE,width=1)))

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
Section 5 · Edible Oil Imports (HS 15)


Code
oil_imp <- mqty[HSCode2 == 15, .(Qty=sum(Import,na.rm=TRUE)), by=Year]

p_oil <- ggplot(oil_imp, aes(x=Year, y=Qty, group=1,
                               text=paste0("Year: ",Year,"\n",format(Qty,big.mark=",")," K Tonnes"))) +
  geom_line(color="#E67E22", linewidth=1.3) +
  geom_point(color="#E67E22", size=3) +
  geom_area(fill="#E67E22", alpha=0.15) +
  geom_smooth(method="lm", se=TRUE, color="#2C3E50", linetype="dashed", linewidth=0.8) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=comma_format()) +
  my_theme +
  labs(title="India's Vegetable Fats & Edible Oil Imports",
       subtitle="Upward structural trend; dashed = linear fit",
       y="Quantity (000 Tonnes)", x="Year",
       caption="Source: DGCI&S | HS Chapter 15 | Analysis: Pawan")

ggplotly(p_oil, tooltip="text") |> layout(hovermode="x unified")

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
text:
Section 6 · Petroleum Imports (HS 27)


Code
petro <- mqty[HSCode2 == 27]
petro[, Commodity := fcase(
  HSCode4=="2701", "Coal",
  HSCode4=="2709", "Crude Petroleum",
  HSCode4=="2710", "Refined Products",
  HSCode4=="2711", "Petroleum Gases",
  default          = "Other Petroleum"
)]
petro_agg <- petro[, .(Qty=sum(Import,na.rm=TRUE)), .(Year, Commodity)]
petro_agg[, Total:=sum(Qty),by=Year]
petro_agg[, Share:=round(Qty/Total*100)]
petro_agg[, tt:=paste0("<b>",Commodity,"</b><br>Year: ",Year,
                        "<br>Qty: ",format(round(Qty/1000),big.mark=",")," MT<br>Share: ",Share,"%")]

p_petro <- ggplot(petro_agg, aes(x=Year, y=Qty/1000, fill=Commodity)) +
  geom_bar_interactive(aes(tooltip=tt, data_id=Commodity),
                        stat="identity", position="stack", width=0.75, alpha=0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(label=ifelse(Share<5,"",paste0(Share,"%"))),
            position=position_stack(vjust=0.5), size=2.8, color="white", fontface="bold") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette="Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=comma_format()) +
  my_theme +
  labs(title="India's Petroleum Imports (HS Chapter 27)",
       subtitle="Crude petroleum dominates; coal and gases growing",
       y="Quantity (Million Tonnes)", x="Year",
       caption="Source: DGCI&S | HS Chapter 27 | Analysis: Pawan")

girafe(ggobj=p_petro, width_svg=12, height_svg=6,
       options=list(
         opts_tooltip(css="background:#fff;color:#333;padding:8px;border-radius:5px;border:1px solid #ddd;"),
         opts_hover(css="opacity:0.85;stroke-width:1.5;"),
         opts_sizing(rescale=TRUE,width=1)))

title: India’s Trade Statistics: Exports, Imports & Sectoral Composition
The Edible Oil Trap: India’s Deepening Import Dependency and the Road to Self-Sufficiency
text:
Walk into any kitchen in India — from a modest rural home in Rajasthan to a high-rise apartment in Mumbai — and you will find at least one or two bottles of edible oil. Cooking oil is indispensable to Indian cuisine. It is the medium through which spices bloom, vegetables are sautéed, and puris are fried. Yet for all its culinary centrality, India produces far less edible oil than it consumes. Every year, the country imports between 14 and 16 million tonnes of vegetable oils — spending over USD 15–20 billion in foreign exchange — to bridge this gap. The story of India’s edible oil import dependence is not merely an agricultural policy failure; it is a story of geopolitical vulnerability, household inflation, and a decades-long policy reckoning that has yet to be fully resolved.
This article traces the evolution of India’s edible oil import basket from the oil year 2010–11 to 2023–24, examines the structural factors driving import dependence, explores the concentration risks embedded in India’s current supply chains, and assesses the policy levers available to reduce this dependence over the coming decade.

title: The Edible Oil Trap: India’s Deepening Import Dependency and the Road to Self-Sufficiency
text:
Walk into any kitchen in India — from a modest rural home in Rajasthan to a high-rise apartment in Mumbai — and you will find at least one or two bottles of edible oil. Cooking oil is indispensable to Indian cuisine. It is the medium through which spices bloom, vegetables are sautéed, and puris are fried. Yet for all its culinary centrality, India produces far less edible oil than it consumes. Every year, the country imports between 14 and 16 million tonnes of vegetable oils — spending over USD 15–20 billion in foreign exchange — to bridge this gap. The story of India’s edible oil import dependence is not merely an agricultural policy failure; it is a story of geopolitical vulnerability, household inflation, and a decades-long policy reckoning that has yet to be fully resolved.
This article traces the evolution of India’s edible oil import basket from the oil year 2010–11 to 2023–24, examines the structural factors driving import dependence, explores the concentration risks embedded in India’s current supply chains, and assesses the policy levers available to reduce this dependence over the coming decade.

title: The Edible Oil Trap: India’s Deepening Import Dependency and the Road to Self-Sufficiency
section: The Scale of the Problem: From Self-Sufficiency to Import Addiction
text:
The Scale of the Problem: From Self-Sufficiency to Import Addiction
In the 1980s, India undertook the Technology Mission on Oilseeds (TMO), which dramatically expanded oilseed cultivation and reduced import dependence for a brief period. By the early 1990s, India came close to self-sufficiency in edible oils. But this achievement was short-lived. As incomes rose through the liberalisation era, per capita edible oil consumption began to climb steadily — from roughly 5 kg per capita per year in the 1980s to over 20 kg today. Domestic oilseed area expansion and yield improvements could not keep pace with this consumption surge.
The data from Ministry of Commerce records shows that by 2010–11, India was already importing approximately 8.7 million tonnes of edible oils annually. By 2023–24, this figure had crossed 16.5 million tonnes — an increase of nearly 90% over fourteen years. The import bill grew even faster because the surge in import volumes coincided with a global commodity supercycle and, critically, with the Russia–Ukraine war of 2022, which triggered an extraordinary price spike across all three major oil categories.
India’s self-sufficiency ratio — domestic edible oil availability as a percentage of total requirement — has been stuck below 50% for most of the past two decades. This means India depends on foreign sources for more than half of every litre of cooking oil consumed in the country. This is a structural vulnerability of the first order.

title: The Edible Oil Trap: India’s Deepening Import Dependency and the Road to Self-Sufficiency
section: The Three-Oil Basket: Palm, Soybean, and Sunflower
text:
The Three-Oil Basket: Palm, Soybean, and Sunflower
India’s edible oil imports are dominated by three oil types, each sourced from a distinct geographic corridor and driven by its own demand and price dynamics.

Palm Oil: The Dominant but Declining Force
Palm oil has historically accounted for the largest share of India’s edible oil imports — as high as 79% of total import volumes in 2010–11. This extraordinary dominance reflects palm oil’s cheapness (it is the world’s lowest-cost edible oil per unit of calorie), its versatility in both cooking and food processing, and its ready availability from Indonesia and Malaysia, which together account for roughly 85% of global palm oil production.
By 2023–24, however, palm oil’s share in India’s import basket had declined to approximately 56%. This is not because volumes fell — palm oil imports remained substantial. Rather, it reflects the faster growth of imports of sunflower oil and, to a lesser extent, soybean oil. Indian consumers, particularly in urban areas, have been shifting away from palm oil toward “lighter” oils perceived as healthier. Simultaneously, food manufacturers began diversifying their oil inputs in response to palm oil price volatility.
The geographic concentration of palm oil supply remains a critical risk. Indonesia and Malaysia combined consistently supply over 90% of India’s palm oil imports. Any disruption to their export policy — Indonesia imposed a devastating palm oil export ban in April 2022, triggering a domestic shortage and price spike in India — directly impacts Indian household food costs within weeks.


Soybean Oil: The Volatile Middle Child
Soybean oil occupies a fluctuating share of 15–30% in India’s edible oil import basket, driven largely by South American supply dynamics. Argentina is the primary supplier, typically providing over 70% of India’s soybean oil imports. Brazil plays a secondary role, with its share rising in years when Argentine production is hit by drought or other supply disruptions.
The Argentina–Brazil duopoly creates its own concentration risk. El Niño-related droughts in the La Plata Basin have repeatedly caused sharp reductions in Argentine soybean production, forcing India to source from Brazil at higher prices or accept domestic oil price increases. The 2022–23 season, which saw one of the worst Argentine droughts on record, is a case in point: soybean oil prices in India spiked sharply as Argentine supplies tightened.


Sunflower Oil: The Fast-Growing New Entrant
The most striking structural shift in India’s edible oil import basket is the rise of sunflower oil. For most of the period from 2010–11 to 2019–20, sunflower oil accounted for only 6–14% of imports. By 2023–24, its share had surged to approximately 22%. Indian consumers — particularly health-conscious urban middle-class households — have embraced sunflower oil as a “light” and relatively neutral-flavoured oil suitable for Indian cooking.
The vast majority of India’s sunflower oil imports come from Ukraine and Russia, which together dominate global sunflower oil production and export. India’s growing reliance on this corridor was brutally exposed in February 2022, when Russia’s invasion of Ukraine triggered an immediate supply disruption. Ukraine’s Black Sea export corridors were blockaded; sunflower oil prices tripled within weeks. India scrambled to source alternative supplies, but the episode underscored a fundamental vulnerability: India had allowed its sunflower oil supply to become almost entirely dependent on a single geopolitically volatile corridor.

title: The Edible Oil Trap: India’s Deepening Import Dependency and the Road to Self-Sufficiency
section: The 2021–22 Price Shock: A Stress Test of India’s Oil Supply Chain
text:
The 2021–22 Price Shock: A Stress Test of India’s Oil Supply Chain
The financial year 2021–22 served as a brutal stress test for India’s edible oil supply chains. A confluence of three simultaneous shocks drove edible oil prices to multi-decade highs. First, the pandemic-era demand recovery in major consuming countries pushed global edible oil demand sharply upward. Second, poor palm oil harvests in Malaysia due to labour shortages (the plantation sector relies heavily on migrant workers who had returned home during COVID) reduced global supply. Third, and most critically, Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in February 2022 disrupted both sunflower oil supplies from the Black Sea corridor and soybean production in Argentina (via knock-on commodity market effects).
India’s total edible oil import bill crossed USD 20 billion in 2021–22 — an extraordinary expenditure that represented a severe drain on foreign exchange reserves and contributed directly to consumer price inflation. The retail price of mustard oil in Delhi touched ₹200 per litre; palm oil, the cheapest staple, crossed ₹140 per litre. For low-income households, which spend 30–40% of their food budget on edible oils, this was a severe income shock.
The government responded with emergency measures: reducing import duties on crude edible oils to zero, banning refined edible oil imports (to protect domestic refiners while ensuring crude oil supply), and releasing strategic reserves. These measures provided some relief, but they illustrated the fundamental problem — India had no deep strategic reserve of edible oils (unlike China, which maintains multi-month reserves), and its domestic production base was too small to provide a meaningful buffer.

title: The Edible Oil Trap: India’s Deepening Import Dependency and the Road to Self-Sufficiency
section: Why Domestic Production Has Failed to Keep Pace
text:
Why Domestic Production Has Failed to Keep Pace
The failure of Indian oilseed production to meet domestic demand is rooted in multiple structural constraints. First, oilseed crops in India are predominantly rainfed and grown on marginal land, with yields far below global averages. India’s soybean yield is approximately 1.0–1.2 MT per hectare — roughly half the Brazilian level and a fraction of the US level. Mustard yields are similarly low by international standards.
Second, oilseed cultivation is in direct competition with cereals — primarily wheat and paddy — which are supported by far more robust procurement, price support, and input subsidy systems. Farmers have a strong rational incentive to grow wheat and paddy, which can be sold to government agencies at guaranteed MSP prices, over oilseeds, which must be sold in private markets at more volatile prices.
Third, the oil palm cultivation programme, which could theoretically provide the highest oil-per-hectare yields of any crop (~4–5 MT of oil per hectare versus 0.3–0.5 MT for soybean), has never scaled to anything approaching its potential. The National Mission on Edible Oils — Oil Palm (NMEO-OP), launched in 2021 with a target of expanding oil palm area to 10 lakh hectares by 2025–26, faces challenges of long gestation periods (oil palms take 5–7 years to reach commercial production), agro-climatic constraints, and the lack of a reliable cold-chain and processing infrastructure in potential cultivation areas like Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, and the Northeast.

title: The Edible Oil Trap: India’s Deepening Import Dependency and the Road to Self-Sufficiency
section: Policy Levers and the Road Ahead
text:
Policy Levers and the Road Ahead
Reducing India’s edible oil import dependence requires action on three fronts simultaneously. On the supply side, India must invest far more aggressively in oilseed research, irrigation coverage for oilseed-growing regions, and procurement support for oilseed farmers. Raising soybean and mustard yields to even 60–70% of global best-practice levels would dramatically expand domestic production. Scaling up the NMEO-OP and solving its implementation bottlenecks is critical for long-term supply expansion.
On the demand side, India should encourage a gradual shift toward more diverse dietary fats, including domestic groundnut oil, sesame oil, and coconut oil, which are produced domestically in sufficient quantities for regional consumption. These oils have higher unit costs but also higher nutritional value and domestic supply resilience.
On the trade policy side, India should build a strategic edible oil reserve of at least 2–3 months of national consumption, financed through a modest cess on edible oil imports. Such a reserve would provide a meaningful buffer against the kind of supply shock that caused the 2021–22 price crisis, buying time for diplomatic and market interventions to take effect.

title: The Edible Oil Trap: India’s Deepening Import Dependency and the Road to Self-Sufficiency
text:
Conclusion
India’s edible oil import dependency is the product of decades of policy choices that prioritised cereal self-sufficiency over oilseed production — a choice that made sense in the food-insecure India of the 1960s and 1970s but has left the country structurally exposed to global commodity markets and geopolitical shocks in the twenty-first century. The 2021–22 oil price crisis was a warning. The window to act — through aggressive oilseed production enhancement, oil palm expansion, and strategic reserve creation — is open, but it will not remain so indefinitely. India’s kitchen economy, feeding 1.4 billion people three meals a day, deserves a more resilient foundation.

Data sources: Ministry of Commerce, India (import statistics); Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare (oilseed production and availability data); Solvent Extractors’ Association of India. Analysis by Pawan.
